responseSet.title: Anxiety as Symptom and Signal.: Edited by Steven P. Roose and Robert A. Glick. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1995. Pp. 182.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anxiety as Symptom and Signal.

Edited by Steven P. Roose and Robert A. Glick. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1995. Pp. 182.

Barbara Milrod

Barbara Milrod

Barbara Milrod
445 East 68th Street, Suite 3N
New York, NY 10028

Barbara Milrod  

Anxiety as Symptom and Signal is a compilation of eleven essays that presents various theoretical perspectives on aspects of the phenomenon of anxiety: as a symptom that causes dysfunction, as an adaptive mechanism in evolution and as it arises in all of the various treatments that are described as ‘psychoanalysis’. Although it contains pieces that cover

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1996

850

evolutionary biology, the neurosciences, and genetics and temperament, this is a book edited by psychoanalysts for psychoanalysts. The goal of the book, as described in the preface, is to acquaint the analyst/reader with interdisciplinary approaches to anxiety, with the idea that in order for psychoanalysis ‘to continue to be both a relevant theory of the mind and a clinically effective treatment’ (p. ix), such an interchange of ideas is essential.

The book contains a large, though not exhaustive, amount of information from different fields. The editors have succeeded in communicating the current range of our understanding of anxiety in one volume. What is also high-lighted is how difficult it will be to integrate these different models. As Morton Reiser notes in the epilogue, ‘For the most part we are faced with two incompatible (biological and mental) domains of data, causing pragmatic observational problems’ (p. 171). Further, as Scott Dowling implies in his lovely essay on the ontogeny and dynamics of anxiety in child-hood, the various authors and the perspectives that they represent in the text do not define ‘anxiety’ in the same way at all. He argues that what is described as ‘anxiety’ in animals is better labelled ‘undifferentiated unpleasure of distress’ (p. 83), and that ‘Human anxiety awaits the formation of mental representations and a rudimentary differentiation of self from other’. In reading this book, it is clear that not even all of the psychoanalysts agree.

Unfortunately, this is a very uneven book, which would have benefited from a stronger editorial hand. Some contributions are elegant, clear and illuminating. Myron Hofer's discussion of evolutionary perspectives, Dowling's developmental description, which is the best concise essay I have read outlining modern ego-psychological perspectives on anxiety, and Steven Roose's paper about medicating patients in psychoanalysis are good examples. Some of the other contributions are disorganised and feature vague clinical examples, making mutual exchange of ideas difficult.

responseSet.title: Panel Report: Psychic Reality And Perversions: Chaired by HENRY SMITH, Cambridge, MA
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: Psychic Reality And Perversions

Chaired by HENRY SMITH, Cambridge, MA

Betty Denzler

Betty Denzler

Betty Denzler
6 route de la Montagne
2023 Gougier
Switzerland

Betty Denzler  

The Chairman opened the panel and drew attention to the differences between the panellists, regarding their theories as well as their patients and styles.

The first contributor, Lee Grossman, spoke on ‘Psychic reality and reality testing in the analysis of perverse defences’. He distinguished between patients with an intact reality sense, who needed only to be made conscious of a fantasy in order to recognise it as such, and others who avoided testing the reality of their perceptions; his point was therefore that the latter possess a reality sense but refuse to use it, in effect claiming that their psychic reality is just as real as the reality tested. The work of analysis in such cases was consequently less a matter of discovering the patient's psychic reality than of showing him how and why he was actively confusing reality and fantasy. Like the other contributors, Grossman attributed this phenomenon to disavowal, but he also had the original idea that these patients were diverting their attention from undesirable perceptions. In conflictual situations they focused on peripheral, relatively insignificant objects.

While the perversions were the most obvious example of this structure, Grossman thought that they formed part of a much wider range of phenomena, and he therefore preferred to speak of a perverse attitude to reality. This attitude carried the risk of collusion between patient and analyst, in which the latter was likely to take the former's psychic reality to be ‘a kind of reality’. For instance, the fetishist, by obliterating the difference between the penis and the fetish, discredited the meaning of a perception. So the pervert perceived the female genitals clearly but then rejected their discovery.

In his view the pervert was perfectly capable of distinguishing his fantasy from what, in the subject's own judgement, was real. His pathology lay in the permission his conscience granted him to maintain his convictions without verification. Grossman added that, if the analyst so to speak caught the patient in the act, he might acknowledge the facts but would refuse to accept their logical consequences. Such subjects resorted to rationalisation to justify their behaviour—a trick that allowed them to deceive themselves. Owing to this danger of misuse of the concept of psychic reality, the analyst needed to seek actively to discover what reality the two parties could agree on.

Panel Report The Expression Of Emotions: A Contemporary Psychoanalytic Perspective

Chaired by JOY OSOFSKY, New Orleans

Michèle d'Épinay

Michèle d'Épinay

Michèle Lalive d'Épinay
4 rue de l'Athen
é
e
1205 Geneva

Michèle d'Épinay  

The Chairman introduced the panellists. She reminded us that the IPA had decided to spend two afternoon sessions of its conference on the subject of research and stressed the importance of research in producing a broader prospective in theoretical and clinical fields, and for the acknowledgement of psychoanalysis itself.

The first panellist, György Gergely, presented his bio-feedback model of parental affect-mirroring. This model was developed in close co-operation with John Watson at the University of California at Berkeley (Gergely & Watson, 1995) to account for the developmental processes leading to the emergence of self-awareness and control in infancy. In this model it is hypothesised that the psychological mechanism involved in affect-mirroring is the same kind of process as is demonstrated in bio-feedback training procedures (Dicara, 1970; Miller, 1969). The current proposal is that parental affect-mirroring provides a kind of natural biofeedback training for the infant that plays a crucial role in emotional development. Raising the question of how externalised representation of the infant's affect-expressions could result in emotional development, Gergely suggested that the mirrored externalised stimulus correlates of the infant's internal state expression were represented at a separate level of secondary representations that became associatively linked to the infant's implicit, non-conscious primary emotional states. Thus, by encoding the affect-mirroring expression of the parent, the infant created a kind of representational redescription (Karmiloff-Smith, 1992) of his implicit automatic emotional states. Unlike the primary emotion programmes, this secondlevel representational structure, based on exteroceptive rather than interoceptive stimuli, was accessible to consciousness and played a vital role in exerting control over primary level automatisms.

Gergely said that the structural characteristics of affect-mirroring were markedness and congruence, and that the markedness referred to the hypothesis that mothers are instinctively driven to saliently ‘mark’ their affect-mirroring displays to make them perceptually differentiable from their realistic emotional expression. He said that this process, called ‘referential decoupling’, permitted the infant to attribute the perceived emotion (for instance an angry or fearful mother)

responseSet.title: Obituary: SAMUEL A. GUTTMAN (1914-1995)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Obituary

SAMUEL A. GUTTMAN (1914-1995)

Stanley Goodman

Stanley Goodman

Stanley Goodman
3736 Highland Road
Lafayette
CA 94549

Stanley Goodman  

Throughout his psychoanalytic career, Sam Guttman was profoundly dedicated to conserving the best of Freudian psychoanalysis while working to advance both its theory and practice. His scientific contributions to the literature and his creation of innovative organisational structures have added a vital stimulating current to the development of psychoanalytic scholarship and education.

At Cornell he gained a doctorate in neurophysiology, biochemistry and anatomy as well as a medical degree. He taught at the University of Pennsylvania Medical School and retired as a professor of psychiatry at Thomas Jefferson University Medical School in Philadelphia. He continued an active clinical practice of psychoanalysis until illness forced his retirement.

He was continually active in local, national and international psychoanalytic organisations, serving as President of the Philadelphia Association for Psychoanalysis, Chairman of the program committee of the American Psychoanalytic Association and Co-Chairman of the program committee of the International Psychoanalytical Association. As a Fellow representing the Philadelphia Association, he was an active and influential force on the Board of Professional Standards of the American Psychoanalytic Association for many years.

His contributions to the psychoanalytic literature included articles on morning depression, bisexuality in symbolism, aspects of scientific theory construction, indications for psychoanalysis, the concept of structure in psychoanalysis, basic concepts of the psychoanalytic point of view and an article on Robert Wälder's work in applying psychoanalysis to social and political phenomena. As literary executor, he also edited a valuable collection of Wälder's papers. He also served for some time on the editorial board of the Psychoanalytic Quarterly.

A major project that he brought to fruition in 1980 is the Concordance to the Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud (Boston: G. K. Hall & Co.). A concordance to the German Gesammelte Werke, a companion project, was published in 1995 (Waterloo, Ontario: North Waterloo Academic Press).1 All psychoanalytic students, teachers and scholars will remain very much in his debt for these efforts, which have created an invaluable resource for Freudian scholarship.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud: From Youthful Dream to Mid-Life Crisis.

By Peter M. Newton. New York & London: The Guilford Press. 1995. Pp. 297.

Nathan Kravis

Nathan Kravis

Nathan M. Kravis
141 East 55th Street
New York, NY 10022

Nathan Kravis  

One might think a subject as thoroughly scrutinised as Freud's life would now offer slim pickings to all but the most polemical biographers. Yet Peter M. Newton, a Professor of Psychology at the Wright Institute in Berkeley, California and a private practitioner, has written an engaging and original book, proving that some lives fruitfully bear endless re-interpretation.

Newton's Freud is informed but not encumbered by the expansive domain of Freud scholarship. His is decidedly not a biography with a huge bibliography or an imposing academic apparatus.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1996

Response

Katherine Dalsimer Ph.D.

Katherine Dalsimer

Katherine Dalsimer, Ph.D.
275 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

Katherine Dalsimer  

Dear Sir:

Dr Kavaler-Adler, in her letter, sails past my review of her book. The various ‘misunderstandings’ she attributes to me concern issues I didn't address. What I did address was her use of theory in ways not sufficiently supple to encompass the complexity of the lives of the writers, or the works of literature, that she analyses.

Yours sincerely
Katherine Dalsimer, Ph.D.

10 June 1996

responseSet.title: Doubts and Certainties in the Practice of Psychotherapy.: By Josephine Klein. London: Karnac Books. 1995. Pp. 290.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Doubts and Certainties in the Practice of Psychotherapy.

By Josephine Klein. London: Karnac Books. 1995. Pp. 290.

Anne Zachary

Anne Zachary

Anne Zachary
13 West Park Road, Kew Gardens, Surrey TW9 4DB

Anne Zachary  

This book has an immediate impact in terms of introducing the life work of a sensitive and experienced clinician who sets out ‘to meet people skin to skin’ (p. 16), and ‘to know the patient rather than to know about him’ (p. 24). The latter is a good way to bring the patient alive, she says, whether in the treatment session or in supervision. Though there is very little biographical information about the author, the reader quickly establishes that she is also a solid and talented teacher.

There is an informality about the style which resembles a personal workbook, a collection of lectures and talks given to a variety of audiences and then recorded for future reference, particularly including her selection and use of other authors. There is a stimulating and controversial bibliography.

The middle section of the book is made up of six chapters on theory and technique. In a style suitable for beginner and experienced alike, she discusses fundamental issues from her own individual perspective, then referring the reader on to a comprehensive list of experts in each field. For example, she describes the overlap between the transference and the therapeutic alliance, suggesting Sodre (1990), ‘Treatment alliances: therapeutic and anti- therapeutic’, for further reading (chapter 7).

The overall message in these chapters concerns assessment, drawing on the valuable teaching and experience of Nina Coltart. There is a salutary reminder to psychoanalysts that not all patients, in fact perhaps very few, are in a position psychologically to be able to adapt immediately if at all to the classic psychoanalytic setting. There are chapters devoted to holding, non-transferential interpretations and transferential interpretations to distinguish one group of patients from another.

The author focuses on the ego and its capacity to instil responsibility for one's self and actions. The doubts of the title include a renunciation of everything being seen as coming from and relevant in the internal world. The ‘world order’ of the external world is given a proper place.

This book, published in the series Opening Out: Feminism for Today, under the general editorship of Teresa Brennan, represents part of the ambition of that series to ‘apply the living insights of feminist critical theory in current social and political contexts’ (p. vi) in order to counter the possibility that feminist scholars are becoming too insulated from the world outside. Psychoanalytic readers should also be aware that several other perspectives—post-modernist, French post-structuralist, critical legal theory, and especially, applied Lacanian psychoanalysis—are here assumed as vantage-points.

Introduction À L'étude De Surmoi [Introduction To The Study Of The Superego].

By Cléopâtre Athanassiou. Lyon: Césura. 1995. Pp. 189.

Elisabeth Hadjiisky

Elisabeth Hadjiisky

Elisabeth Hadjiisky
74 rue Dunois, 75013 Paris

Elisabeth Hadjiisky  

The author, who is a child and adult psychoanalyst and has already written several books, in effect here offers us a serious and painstaking technical treatise. The genesis of the superego in Freud and the contributions of his ‘heirs’ down to the present day are dealt with chronologically. The concept is anchored in the thought of each of the relevant authors, the obscurities in some being illuminated by the ideas of the others. For all the aridity of the subject, the book is easy to read. Freud leaves us with a paradox: how can the superego, as the heir of the Oedipus complex, result from the introjection of an object that is at the same time the bearer of retaliation; and how can it lie at the root of sublimation, and hence of ego maturation, while simultaneously reacting in accordance with the lex talionis? The author's account shows Freud evolving from the first to the second topography.

Athanassiou presumes that Freud's superego has a twofold origin, one aspect of which is narcissistic (primary narcissism) while the other, more mature, is oedipal. The superego includes a severe part, which operates patho logically in melancholia: the ego kills itself in order to punish the introjected object. In the author's view, the severe (or even criminal) superego stems from the omnipotent parts of the self, which inflict punishment in accordance with the lex talionis.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1996

Letter

Jean Roiphe

Jean Roiphe

Jean Roiphe
40 East 83 Street
New York, NY 10028

Jean Roiphe  

Dear Sir,

Perhaps the Tower of Babel looms even larger than I might have imagined. I am perplexed by the degree to which R. Steiner feels I have misrepresented his fascinating paper, ‘“The Tower of Babel” or “After Babel in contemporary psychoanalysis”?’ (1994a), in the few instances where I have represented it at all.

Steiner states in his letter that ‘it does not seem to me that the author [meaning myself], on the basis of her observations, has fully understood what I meant, that is, that there is, to my mind, a danger of failing into a religious frame of mind, even in the empirical and scientific approach to psychoanalysis’. But it is precisely for the elaboration of this idea that I reference his paper twice.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letter

Lee Grossman

Lee Grossman

Lee Grossman
5335 College Avenue, Suite 27
Oakland, California 94618

Lee Grossman  

Dear Sir,

I am grateful to Dr Plaut for his careful reading of my paper. He makes a number of important points, to which I would like to respond:

1. When the analyst confronts the patient with ‘the’ reality, the implication is that the standards of reality ‘should be … the same as the analyst's’. He infers that I assume that the testimony of our senses is an infallible guide to what is real, and that I claim a ‘certitude as to reality and its normalistic or even moralistic aspects’.

I believe neither that perception is infallible, nor that the analyst is the arbiter of what is real and what is not. As I said on the first page (509), perception is an actively interpretive act, rather than a passively receptive one. On the other hand, I do believe that we always assume that we have at least an approximate consensus about certain things being real and certain things being unreal; the assumption that we share a universe of discourse is basic for any communication to be possible. That does not mean that we are right, but it does mean that we are stuck with those judgments. In any case, in the analysis of perverse patients (and most other analytic patients), the issue is rarely that the analyst and patient disagree about what is real. Every fetishist I ever worked with could tell me that a woman does not ‘really’ have a penis. The man in my case example could tell me, when he turned his attention to it (i.e. ‘tested reality’), that he ‘really’ had a mole that ‘really’ had changed—even though he wanted desperately to believe otherwise. Most of us would not call a patient who argued that women have penises, or that the mole was not there, ‘perverse’; we would call such a patient psychotic. The issue with patients who use perverse defenses is not that they disagree with the analyst about what is real, but that they ignore the implications of their own assessment of what is real. When I refer to the analyst as being a spokesperson for reality, I do not advocate arguing with the patient about what is real. I advocate confronting the patient with his or her effort to discount the consequences of his or her own judgment of the situation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Quest for Answers: A Primer of Understanding and Treating Severe Personality Disorders.

By Salman Akhtar. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 222.

Lawrence Rockland

Lawrence Rockland

Lawrence H. Rockland
The New York Hospital
—
Cornell Medical Center
Westchester Division, 21 Bloomingdale Road
White Plains, NY 10605

Lawrence Rockland  

This book consists of a hundred questions related to the aetiology, diagnosis and psychodynamic treatment of severe personality disorders, each responded to by a succinct, theoretically and clinically sophisticated answer. The questions and answers are organised into six Parts: ‘Diagnosis and differential diagnosis’, ‘Structure and dynamics’, ‘Origins and development’, ‘Evaluation and triage’, ‘Psychotherapy and psychoanalysis’ and ‘Prognosis and outcome’. This simple, sensible organisation, together with the author's impressive talents as teacher and writer (he is a published poet) yields a book that rewards the reader with abundant theoretical and/or clinical material on every page, is beautifully written and easy to understand and, most especially, is extremely enjoyable to read.

Part I, ‘Diagnosis and differential diagnosis’, describes the eight personality disorders that the author considers severe. They are the Narcissistic, Borderline, Schizoid, Paranoid, Antisocial, Schizotypal and Histrionic (all DSMIV Axis II diagnoses; they also fit criteria for Kernberg's Borderline Personality Organisation) plus the Hypomanic Personality Disorder, which Akhtar persuasively argues should be included in the group.

Most impressive to me is the author's skill in integrating clinical/descriptive, psychodynamic, psychostructural, developmental and socio-cultural viewpoints. For example,

individuals suffering from these disorders exhibit … chronic restlessness, unstable emotions, vacillating relationships … defective empathy, inability to love [clinical/descriptive] … splitting … is a major defensive operation … accompanied by the subsidiary mechanisms of denial, primitive idealization, and projective identification [psychodynamic] … most important, the lack of an integrated self-concept, resulting in … identity diffusion [psychostructural] … these conditions are associated with an aborted separation-individuation process and the consequent failure to master the Oedipus complex. There is lack of object constancy … and much unneutralized aggression … [developmental] (p. 3).

[[RunningHead]]

La Consignation Du Sublimable [The Placing On Record Of The Sublimable].

By J. M. Porret. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, ‘Le Fil Rouge’, 1994. Pp. 249.

Beatrice Marbeau-Clairens

Beatrice Marbeau-Clairens

Beatrice Marbeau-Clairens
42 rue Notre Dame des Champs, 75006 Paris

Beatrice Marbeau-Clairens  

Serge Lebovici's foreword summarises the book, praises its theoretical rigour and makes one want to read it.

The author begins with a detailed account of the place of sublimation in Freud's first theory of drives. The formation of the drive for knowledge is stated to ensue from the sublimation of the drive for mastery, which itself draws its sexual dimension from the analsadistic drives. There follows a close analysis of Freud's variants, qualifications and second thoughts as applied by him over the years to his initial formulations. The later reintroduction of repression, which he had first excluded from the process of sublimation, is an example of such a modification.

The concept of the ‘aim-inhibited drive’ was introduced in 1921 in the second theories of the drives. The author considers that when the subject becomes aware of the existence of the real father and of the mother's cathexis of him, this gives rise to a ‘decathexis of the trans-narcissistic relationship of the child with the mother's body’, thereby obliterating the manifestations of the child's destructive drives and favouring the emergence of affection. In his comments on Freud's concepts, Porret draws in particular on the work of Laplanche and Green.

Again, any activity that generates structural change is necessarily destructive of at least a part of the former state, so that it assigns to a part of the drive a different aim from its original destructive aim. In the author's view, curiosity, the drive to investigate, can be seen as a complete and successful sublimation of the aggressive drive. Inhibition of the initial aim of the destructive drives is the vehicle whereby the superego eventually performs its function of secondary inhibition of the sexual aim in the sublimation process.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1996

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Inceste Et Jalousie [Incest And Jealousy].

By Denis Vasse. Paris: Seuil. 1995. Pp. 311.

Annie Laquerrière

Annie Laquerrière

Annie Laquerrière
17 rue Commandeur, 75014 Paris

Annie Laquerrière  

The psychoanalyst Denis Vasse has written a number of books that delve deep into the heart of what makes man human. He speaks to us as a dedicated clinical psychoanalyst patiently seeking his truth, which he offers to those who tell him of the distress of having life pass them by. He writes as a Christian, integrating faith and the sacred texts with delicate touches that emphasise the Truth of Man, as in the following quotation from the Old Testament: ‘My soul waiteth for the Lord more than they that watch for the morning’ (Psalm 130, v. 6).

His own complex progression, paralleling the wanderings of the human soul, is filled with emotions that accompany a process of stripping bare which no psychoanalyst reading the book can reject for himself.

In this inevitably brief review, it is impossible to convey all the themes, concepts and notions that are here developed with a precision born of an art of communication, of telling oneself to the other if he is prepared to listen; here, then, is the fundamental subject-matter of this book on jealousy and incest, namely the refusal to speak, which always involves two protagonists, even if the primal attribution of the refusal remains uncertain and although this refusal of course concerns the analysand.

Let us consider in more detail some of the ideas presented. Jealousy carries within itself the need for possession, lying, exclusion, refusal to speak, to share and to give. While rejecting the possibility of an encounter, it incessantly lays claim to it in ways subtly masked by pernicious demands for the possession, or even extinction, of the other; ‘it is either him or me’ then represents the triumph of the jealous subject. The theatre of jealousy stages a primal scene in which the subject wants everything, but without desire, so that he aborts before he is even born. The jealous subject, who seeks his pleasure in the place that is rightfully the other's, is driven by hate. He destroys himself by avoiding the place from which he would be able to speak, which would reveal his own identity to him. Identity and otherness are mocked by perversion of the relationship to speaking. He refuses to become conscious of the lack and thus tends towards an absolute narcissism; avoidance of castration may also be said to be involved.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La Cruauté Mélancolique [The Cruelty Of Melancholia].

By J. Hassoun. Paris: Aubier, 1995. Pp. 129.

Annie Laquerrière

Annie Laquerrière

Annie Laquerrière
17 rue Commandeur 75014 Drancy

Annie Laquerrière  

The psychoanalyst Jacques Hassoun, who has written a number of analytic works, presents a serious and profound examination of the melancholic structure, which is fundamentally relevant to the secret of ontological structure. Inherent in the book is therefore a question that confronts every psychoanalyst, as the author points out in quoting Thomas Mann's statement that psychoanalysis is a melancholic mode of knowledge. This question necessarily includes the passage through death, and it sustains the book at each of its intersections.

The author adopts a rigorously clinical approach, with references to Karl Abraham, Freud and Lacan; each successive example delves deeper into the very core of the malady of melancholia, which is exposed as downfall, obscenity, apathy, inertia, self-aversion, passive resistance, rottenness, diminution, unworthiness and a lust born of humiliation, all of which are expressions of the melancholic's cruelty to himself and others.

For Jacques Hassoun, the core of melancholia is the murder of the Thing, defined as ‘the primordial object that is lost for ever, yet in a way never entirely lost, because the subject will always be concerned to find it again’. Considered in these terms, the symbolic may be seen as murder of the Thing. The author thus constructs a theory of melancholia based on the relation to the object, taking account of the missing lost Thing, which is never lost, which is to be lost—in other words, of the impossibility of mourning. Hence the melancholic is ‘at all times under the yoke of a necessity, namely the murder of what strikes at the very heart of his subjectivity, the slow murder of those he loves’, after which he encounters the inner desert and feels that the obvious course is suicide. Emergence from melancholia through psychoanalysis entails a position in which the Thing that has escaped is commuted into murder. The same passage also has to be negotiated by any psychic organisation that is called upon to accept the processes of symbolisation, the relationship with the Law, temporality and so on.

[[RunningHead]]

La Consolation [Consolation].

By J. Hochmann. Paris: Odile Jacob. 1994. Pp. 328.

Denise Weil

Denise Weil

Denise Weil
31 rue Claude Bernard, 75005 Paris

Denise Weil  

This book by Jacques Hochmann, professor of psychiatry and psychoanalyst (Member of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society), with its fine title La consolation, is an essay on mental health care. It is both a meditation and a reflection on the practice of psychiatry and on the influences to which it and the author himself have been exposed in the particularly fruitful period of the last thirty years. This journey is examined with a critical eye, revealing a man of great culture and humanity. As a neurologist and psychoanalyst who has explored the new Californian therapies, and a committed protagonist of community psychiatry, he knows the reality of institutional practices and typical forms of treatment, and therefore distrusts all forms of dogmatism. His route is illustrated at every stage by histories of patients as persons, rather than ‘clinical vignettes’, in which he himself is involved; they are punctuated with his reflections and indeed his self-analysis. The diary kept by the author during an extreme experience with psychopaths is a rare document. Above all, however, in proceeding in this way he dismantles not only simplifying ideologies but also improper conceptualisations, demonstrating the underlying countertransference manifestations.

Some of the chapters bear witness to the author's original experience—of working with families in their own homes, of the centre open to psychopaths, of his work with autistic patients and of his approach to families. However, the book as a whole revolves around the central question of psychoanalysis and psychiatry. The chapter on this subject raises the question of the psychotic transference, which, the author claims—‘shocking as it may sound’ —is not possible: ‘There is no psychoanalysis of the psychoses’. To illustrate his argument and to demonstrate his approach, he invites us to reread ‘Gradiva’. He is then able to tell us that ‘care is a tale’, the tale that confers meaning on what the patient has experienced and continues to experience with his therapist, on the basis of a shared reality in each case.

7 rue des Blancs Manteaux, 75004 Paris

Perel Wilgowicz  

Three authors, Freud, Lacan and Leclaire, are quoted in the epigraph to this ambitious and highly topical book. The psychoanalyst and novelist Marc Nacht, with subtle and delicate strokes of his alert pen, delves deeper and deeper on every page in his investigation of the death drive and the explosion and violence of barbarity in contemporary societies. His approach to their subterranean workings is by way of the interlinking of the relevant social and individual factors. Trauma and its repetition are examined in the light of Freud's conviction of the existence of a transgenerational transmission of the terrors of mankind through a phylogenetic heritage.

In Julien Gracq's short story Une communication exquise, the junior officer Grange, called to the colours in 1939 after the Munich agreement, has the revelation when about to die that he is ‘dwelling like a tenant who does not know when his lease expires’. Pursuing a parallel between the Freudian death drive as an attempt to put an end to existential tensions and Lacan's ‘jouissance‘ [enjoyment], Nacht endeavours to track down in clinical practice the effects of the shadows of history on a subject's mind; these have ‘the hardness of a foreign body’, whose ‘density and invisibility call to mind the ancient stars known as black holes’. In this strange or even uncanny transmission of fantasies or behaviour, the author sees the emergence of the Lacanian real (defined as something that hinders psychic processes), expressing the tendency towards the inert and trauma, which has the two aspects of, firstly, a source of tension that gives rise to a greater complexity and, secondly, a weakening of the vital reactions and a progression towards death. The theory of repetition and primal trauma are ‘an overdetermining factor upstream of the traumatic component of infantile sexuality’. Through trauma, the Freudian concept of a phylogenetic heritage initiates the attempt to ‘think the unthinkable’. Anxiety and nostalgia for an earlier past—of a paradise lost ‘prior to the break constituted by representation’—ultimately lead to ‘the helplessness of experiencing a discontinuity’. Repression, memory and remembering are restored in the treatment to the context of a personalised reprsentability that combines collective and personal history.

responseSet.title: Psychic Reality And Perversions
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychic Reality And Perversions

Lee Grossman

Lee Grossman

Lee Grossman
5335 College Avenue, Ste. 27
Oakland, California 94618

Lee Grossman  

Dear Sir:

I would like to clarify a point in Betty Denzler's report on the panel ‘Psychic reality and perversions’ (IJPA, 77: 61-66). Dr Denzler reported that ‘Grossman's words suggest that he regarded [perverse] patients as dishonest, although he denied this when asked’ (p. 66). If Dr Denzler heard me as denying that suggestion, then I did not make my views plain. As I indicated in the full text of my paper (abbreviated for presentation), I do in fact regard the pervasive disavowal of reality that I call perverse defenses as a form of dishonesty. In fact, I would suggest that ‘the pervasive disavowal of reality’ is a pretty good definition of dishonesty.

Dr Denzler seemed to think I was calling perverse patients immoral when she wrote: ‘The seemingly immoral attitude of perverse patients … is … a response to … anxieties’. But I was not equating dishonesty with immorality. From the perspective of the analyst, the morality of dishonesty is irrelevant. The dishonesty I refer to is perceiving something to be one way and then deceiving oneself in order to act as if it were otherwise—and I understand that to be motivated by anxiety or other psychic pain. We do not take up dishonesty in analysis because it is immoral; we take it up when it interferes with optimal functioning, and we take it up in order to help with the underlying pain.

Dishonesty is not exempt from psychological understanding just because it has a pejorative meaning in social usage. Neither our capacity for empathy nor our willingness to see things as they are should be abandoned when we encounter dishonesty in our patients.

I hope the forthcoming publication of the full text of my paper will make my thoughts on the matter more accessible.

Sincerely,
Lee Grossman

1233

responseSet.title: IJPA On The Internet And CD-Rom
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

IJPA On The Internet And CD-Rom

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

David Tuckett  

From 1 October this year the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis will have a presence on the Internet. Early in 1997 the first 75 volumes of the Journal will also be available on a CD-ROM disc. Both developments will offer greatly enhanced opportunities for the growth and development of the Journal and our discipline.

From 1 October Visit our Home Page at http://www.ijpa.org/ijpa/

The internet is a global network of computers that allows rapid communication among millions of estimated users. One of its most important innovations has been the creation of the World Wide Web, which allows users to exchange not only text but also images and sound. Documents are stored on what are known as ‘home pages’ at ‘web sites’ and are available for the price of a local telephone call to anyone with a computer, a modem, and a subscription contract to an internet provider. Two years ago there were only about 100 web sites. Today there are probably well over 50,000. From 1 October the International Journal will be one of these sites—launching itself into so-called cyberspace. We believe the consequences will be far-reaching.

Users will now be able to scan the contents list of forthcoming issues of the Journal, pick out abstracts that interest them, and download them in full. One or more articles from each issue will be available in full text, as will all forthcoming book reviews and panel reports from IPA congresses. All these may also be downloaded and printed out or stored on the user's own computer. In time, whole past issues of the journal will be accessed in this way.

The site will also provide a full listing of all psychoanalytic books received in all major languages, full information about subscriptions, announcements, details about how to submit papers and ‘buttons’ providing ‘one click’ access to other sites of likely interest to users. Because an internet site can be maintained daily, information of this kind will always be up to date.

Putting the Journal on the internet will make it more widely available, especially to people in countries and situations where a combination of economics and local facilities have barred access to psychoanalysis and psychoanalytic journals. Telecommunications systems worldwide are being updated and it is widely expected that in many places access to the internet will be easier than access to psychoanalytic journals in libraries.

responseSet.title: The Birth of Hatred.: Edited by Salman Akhtar, Selma Kramer and Henri Parens. Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 172.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Birth of Hatred.

Edited by Salman Akhtar, Selma Kramer and Henri Parens. Northvale, New Jersey: Jason Aronson. 1995. Pp. 172.

Allan Compton

Allan Compton

Allan Compton
1081 Marage Drive, Los Angeles, CA 90049

Allan Compton  

The eight chapters in this book were originally papers presented at the Twenty-Fifth Annual Margaret Mahler Symposium in April 1994. There are chapter-specific bibliographies and a final index in common. Kramer provides a general introduction to the topic, and Neubauer a concluding summary. Chapters by Blum, Kernberg and Pine are discussed, respectively, by Parens, Akhtar, and Holmes. The sub-title ‘Developmental, Clinical and Technical Aspects of Intense Aggression’ better captures the content than does the title ‘The Birth of Hatred’. There is no unifying concept of hate that carries across the chapter boundaries, which may produce stimulation or confusion for various readers.

Selma Kramer focuses on aggression between parents and children, with strong emphasis on aggression of parents towards children. She sees, for example, sending young men to war as an expression of filicidal impulses of ‘fathers who hate’ and of ‘parental hostility towards children’. There is no effort to differentiate the concepts of aggression, hate and hostility.

Harold Blum makes an expansive effort to consider aggression, destructive aggression, hostility and hate, focusing mostly on social and developmental aspects. He mentions Freud's 1915 delineation of love and hate (‘Instincts and their vicissitudes’, S.E. 14) as attitudes of the ‘ego’ towards objects, but does not give this formulation any central position. Henri Parens, in his discussion of Blum's chapter, gives serious attention to trying to define the various terms which are central to the book. Parens wonders whether hate is to be viewed as a drive or an affect, and takes the following position: ‘Hate is on the spectrum of hostile destructive affects that constitute the hostile destructive current (trend) in aggression … It is the experience of excessive unpleasure that activates rage, hostility and eventually hate’ (p. 44). Unlike Blum, Parens sees hate and love as ‘counterparts’.

Kernberg, in his chapter, ‘Hatred as a core affect of aggression’, presents the most comprehensive and unified theory of the topic, in terms of his long developing theory of affect-self and object units as the basic building blocks of mental structure. In this conceptualisation, libidinal and aggressive drives are secondary to the basic affects, which Kernberg here presents as rage and elation. The primitive crying response evolves into the affect of rage; aggression develops out of rage (p. 59); hatred is a ‘a complex structured derivative of the affect rage, that expresses several wishes ...’ (p. 64). Kernberg also addresses the problem of physical and sexual abuse and treatment issues. Salman Akhtar, in his discussion of Kernberg's chapter, challenges several aspects of the theory presented. He notes the asymmetry of Kernberg's formulations of sexual and aggressive drives. He then calls attention to other formulations of difference between sexuality and aggression, in particular Anna Freud's suggestion that aggressive aims associate themselves with varying purposes, while libidinal aims are specific to the drive.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: Origin Of Psychic Reality In The Child

Chaired by DIETER BRGIN, Basel

James Herzog

James Herzog

James M. Herzog
75 Intervale Road
Newton Centre
MA 02159

James Herzog  

The Chairman opened the panel and spoke about the triadic framework, both pre-oedipal and oedipal, in which the origins of psychic reality are embedded. Citing Peter Fonagy's remark in the morning panel that Descartes's ‘cogito ergo sum’ is not the basis of psychic reality, Bürgin suggested that Daniel Stern's concept of the pre-narrative envelope might be more germane. Bürgin's focus was on an object-relations perspective and ways in which the child begins to construct a story about himself with his significant others.

Drawing upon work with a young boy, Peter, that was conducted in the presence of his mother, the first panellist, Douwe Jongbloed, asserted that ‘sometimes it is the task of the analyst to help a child in establishing a transitional psychic reality, since that is a precondition for the development, for the acceptance of an internal psychic reality. The two seem to run together: the interpretation and articulation of what belongs to that internal psychic reality also helps to develop a transistional sphere. There seems to be a developmental conjunction here: the one is both cause and consequence of the other. Together with this goes the awareness and acceptance that other persons have internal psychic realities as well’.

Jongbloed's patient is helped to play and to achieve a ‘make-believe’ quality in this activity. The great Dutch historian Huizenga is quoted to emphasise this transition. A father returns home and kisses his 4-year-old son who is playing trains with chairs. The boy responds: ‘Don't kiss the engine, Daddy, or the carriage won't think it's real’. Jongbloed asserts that interpreting wishes and fears about aggression assists in the construction of an intermediate space and thus in the establishment of transitional psychic reality. With his patient, Peter, and in the presence of Peter's mother, Jongbloed interprets both in words and in attitude and action his conviction that the little boy can endure separation and tolerate intense affect. The encounter with the analyst as a survivor of aggression helps the child to see that he, too, can manage this affect.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Conceptualisation And Communication Of Clinical Facts

R. Steiner

R. Steiner

R. Steiner
12A Belsize Lane
London NW3 5AB

R. Steiner  

Sir,

I would like to make a few brief comments, clarifications and corrections vis-à-vis some statements contained in J. Roiphe's paper ‘The conceptualisation and communication of clinical facts’ (International Journal of Psycho-Analysis, 76: 1179-1190).

On page 1179 of her paper the author writes: ‘It is interesting and informative (as Steiner, 1994, elaborates) that Tuckett invokes the discourse of science, while using as his central metaphor for the volume the language of religion’. If I understand correctly, reference is being made here to Tuckett's image of the Babel of ideas within contemporary psychoanalysis, and to the Tower of Babel (see Tuckett, ‘Foreword’, International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 75, p. 865). She also refers to my paper in the same issue, entitled, ‘The Tower of Babel, or “After Babel in contemporary psychoanalysis”’, (Steiner, 1994a, pp. 883-901).

The author seems to imply that I have elaborated on Tuckett's ‘Foreword’, and in particular, his metaphors. As a point of information I should perhaps mention that I was asked to give the title of my paper to the Editor of the International Journal of Psycho-Analysis a few months before the delivery of my paper at West Point in April 1994. I read Tuckett's ‘Foreword’ only after its publication in the Journal. I did not discuss his ‘Foreword’ with him, and only very briefly mentioned the content of my paper once during an informal conversation with him, just to give him some idea of what I intended to say. Indeed, due to various pressures I was unable to complete my paper in time for it to be circulated in advance. The Editor allowed me to present it at West Point after reading it during the flight to New York. I did not, therefore, elaborate on his ‘Foreword’.

Sometimes, in order to achieve accurate conclusions, it is a good idea to try using cumulative evidence. This can be done by employing empirical methods that eschew first sight evidence. This goes as much for reading and interpreting, including thinking about papers written by one's colleagues, as for any other activity. It is a useful precaution not to use indicative tenses when making statements about other people when one is not absolutely certain that they are correct. It is of course impossible to achieve complete objectivity in this area, but without the precautions I have just mentioned one is bound to project too massively one's own personal viewpoint, not to mention all sorts of other implications, into the text in question.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Review Of Het Geval Freud: 1. Scheppingsverhalen.

By Han Israëls: Uitgeverij Bert Bakker. 1993. Pp. 248.

Mark Solms

Mark Solms

Mark Solms
4B Hampstead Hill Gardens
London NW3 2PL

Mark Solms  

This book, the title of which translates literally as ‘The Case of Freud: 1. Stories of Creation’, focuses on Freud's early research into the subjects of cocaine and hysteria. However, the book is not a serious attempt to document and appraise this period in Freud's working life; rather it is a piece of investigative journalism which leaves no stone unturned in an attempt to discredit the man Sigmund Freud. In this respect it belongs to the currently fashionable genre of literature devoted to the assassination of the character and scientific integrity of the founder of psychoanalysis.

When a deceased scientist is attacked in this way, and he is no longer able to defend himself, it falls to the lot of those who know better to ensure that historical and scientific injustices do not go unchallenged. Otherwise a caricature can gradually replace the complex reality in the minds of uninformed readers, and sometimes even readers of the specialist literature too. This responsibility is particularly pressing in instances where the published allegations are propagated further by uncritical book reviews in serious newspapers, the readers of which are in no position to form an independent judgement on the matter (see for example, in the present instance, Martin Stingelin's review in the Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung of 30 June 1993).

Isaëls's book was brought to my attention by the Director of Sigmund Freud Copyrights, who had given the author access to restricted documents in his care. My own expertise related mainly to the first section of the book, the part on cocaine. I was asked to offer an opinion on the standard of scholarship in this part, before publication, so that Sigmund Freud Copyrights could decide whether or not to grant the necessary permissions.

The standard of scholarship was extremely poor. Like so many other recent examples in this genre, the book was riddled with errors and half-truths which became the basis for far-reaching conclusions. In the present case, the conclusions were quite devastating: Freud was an unprincipled man, driven by boundless ambition, prepared to do anything to advance his personal fame—cheat in his research, lie in his publications, libel his colleagues, knowingly recommend dangerous treat ments, and even kill his friends (while stealing their money in the process).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report - Psychic Reality: Theoretical Concepts

Chaired by CHARLES BRENNER, New York

Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams
Flat 1
15 Rosslyn Hill
London NW3 5UJ

Paul Williams  

The Chairman opened the panel by introducing the panellists and their background pre-published papers. Britton proposed that ‘belief is to psychic reality what perception is to material reality’, so that belief confers the force of reality to that which is psychic in the sense that ‘seeing is believing’. Like perceiving, believing is active and is influenced by desire, fear and expectation. Therefore, perceptions can be denied in the same way that beliefs can. He then distinguished between belief and knowledge, describing the latter as based on objective evaluation. To become knowledge, in this sense, belief requires sensory confirmation (reality testing). In the absence of objective confirmation, belief may confer the status of reality on mental products, including fantasies, with the important consequences that led Freud to introduce the concept of psychic reality in the first place. As an alternative to denial, defences such as counter-belief, the annihilation of belief and the suspension of belief may be used. Counter-belief lies at the heart of defensive organisations and defends the patient from believing existing fantasies.

Britton described a female patient who it seemed firmly believed she had a special relationship with her analyst/father, a belief that she used to counter fantasies of exclusion. Another female patient maintained a counter-belief that she would go blind if she lost sight of her mother. Britton suggested that the annihilation of belief is a drastic schizoid defence against feelings of impending catastrophe and can yield a sense of futility and emptiness, often accompanied by de-realisation. Suspension of belief, found in extreme form in the as-if personality, is relatively common in analysis. An example was given of a male patient who suspended a belief in the apparent coldness and sadism of his analyst by idealising him and by somatising affective pain. Revelation of the patient's belief in his unconscious fantasies was a prerequisite to his being able to test them and relinquish them.

[[RunningHead]]

Key Figures in Counselling and Psychotherapy. Series Editor: Windy Dryden. D. W. Winnicott.

By Michael Jacobs. London: Sage Publications. 1995. Pp. 163.

Helen Robinson

Helen Robinson

Helen Taylor Robinson
4 Whellock Road, London W4 1DZ

Helen Robinson  

This slim volume is designed as part of a series of books introducing the readership of counsellors and psychotherapists to leading innovators in their field, aiming to provide a comprehensive overview of their theory and contributions. This is an excellent aim. D. W. Winnicott now joins the previous series figures of Freud, Klein, Rogers, Perls, Berne, Beck, Ellis, Wolpe and Kelly, in Michael Jacobs's account of his theory and practice, his particular presentation of Winnicott's major contributions to each. As well as this, Jacobs includes a brief biography, an attempt at an appraisal of critical views and, finally, an assessment of Winnicott's overall influence.

In reading this volume what needs to be kept in mind is that this concise examination of Winnicott's position is in relation to the field of counselling and psychotherapy per se, and not specifically, or only incidentally, to the fields of paediatrics, psychoanalysis, child psychology and psychiatry, child development, child care and education, to which, among others, Winnicott may be said to have contributed.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Essential Other. A Developmental Psychology of the Self.

By Robert M. Galatzer-Levy and Bertram J. Cohler. New York: Basic Books. 1993. Pp. 468.

Morton Shane and Estelle Shane

Estelle Shane

Morton Shane
Estelle Shane
1800 Fairburn Avenue, Los Angeles, CA 90025

Estelle Shane  

This long, ambitious work recasts psychoanalytic developmental theory in terms of the necessity for the individual to experience essential others throughout the course of life; others, that is, needed for adequate growth not only in infancy, childhood, and adolescence, but equally in young adulthood, middle age, and late life. This theme is repeated, elaborated, integrated, and illustrated in every chapter. Galatzer-Levy & Cohler are obviously inspired by Kohut's selfobject concept, as well as by Kohut's contention that selfobjects are required throughout the life span, but they deliberately expand upon these constructs. They argue that the selfobject concept has not been sufficiently delineated, either by Kohut himself, or by the other self-psychologists who have followed him.

responseSet.title: Anthropology and Psychoanalysis: an Encounter Through Culture.: Edited by Suzette Heald and Ariane Deluz. London: Routledge. 1994. Pp. 244.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Anthropology and Psychoanalysis: an Encounter Through Culture.

Edited by Suzette Heald and Ariane Deluz. London: Routledge. 1994. Pp. 244.

Rosine Perelberg

Rosine Perelberg

Rosine Jozef Perelberg
35 Hodford Road,
 London NW11 8NL

Rosine Perelberg  

The interrelationship between psychoanalysis and anthropology is as old as the discipline of psychoanalysis itself. Throughout his work Freud attempted to establish connections between the two disciplines in his attempt to validate the universality of his theories (especially 1911, 1921). Early in the development of social anthropology, however, the individual became excluded from the field of analysis. I suggest that this inaugurated a series of dichotomies, namely those between Nature and Culture in the French tradition, Personality and Culture in America and Right and Sentiment in the British tradition.

Durkheim inaugurated the tradition where-by ‘social facts’ should be studied as ‘things’ and should thus be perceived as independent of and external to the conceptual apparatus of both the individual and the observer. This is linked to his attempts to delineate a field of knowledge opposed to psychology and biology. Several dichotomies, such as those between social/individual, moral rules/sensual appetites, concepts/sensations, sacred/profane and normal/pathological, are derived from this framework.

In England, since in his analysis of the ‘familiar complex’ among the Trobriand Islanders Malinowski claimed that the fundamental opposition in this society was between ‘mother right’ and ‘father love’, the British tradition in anthropology has been trapped in a debate between ‘rights’ against ‘sentiments’ and the systems whereby these are transmitted along different lines in traditional societies. This dichotomy implies questions about the role of sentiment in social life and the space allocated to the individual in the analytical model of British social anthropology. Radcliffe-Brown's famous article on the mother's brother is also at the centre of this opposition between right and sentiment (1924). In this paper he argued that in matrilineal societies the father represents authority and the mother sentiments, and the mother's brother is identified with the mother. For Turner (1969) this became transformed into the opposition between structure and communitas and for Fortes into the opposition between the domestic and the politico-jural domains (1969). The former generates sentiments which are limited by natural constraints, in contrast with the social values of the politico-jural domain. In all these distinctions, the line of sentiments was conceived as a type of psychological phenomenon distinct from and opposing social institutions.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L'idéal Du Moi Dans Le Contre-Transfert [The Ego Ideal In The Countertransference].

By T. H. Fua. With a foreword by P. Ferrari. Lyon: Césura. 1994. Pp. 192.

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin  

This engaging but at the same time disconcerting little book by a colleague extols the analyst's ego ideal as the ‘guarantor of creativity and proportion in analytic treatment’. What we are offered is a dialogue, or rather an inner ballet, as the analyst's listening encounters the watchful superego of an ‘eminent colleague’, whose admonitions are foiled by ‘Nathan the Wise’, a figure identified with the analyst's inner freedom, the foundation of which is his ego ideal. As the enemy of the status quo and the guarantor of an unfailing presence, Nathan the Wise allows the analyst to indulge in a degree of madness, or at least grants him a level of imagination and freedom capable of withstanding the pitfalls of the transference relationship and of permitting the unexpected ‘breakthroughs’ whereby some treatments progress.

This somewhat demonstrative rhetoric is fleshed out when the author relates it to her own analytic journey, in which such figures as Sacha Nacht, Didier Anzieu and Anne Clancier, as well as André Green and Jeannine Kalmanovitch, play prominent parts. However, we also encounter the dancer Laban and the biblical injunction lekha (‘get thee out of thy country’) that called upon Abraham to quit Ur for the land of Canaan; these too may be found on her route towards inner freedom.

responseSet.title: Sandor Rado And Adolf Meyer: A Nodal Point In American Psychiatry And Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sandor Rado And Adolf Meyer: A Nodal Point In American Psychiatry And Psychoanalysis

Craig Tomlinson

Craig Tomlinson

Craig Tomlinson
New York Hospital
—
Cornell Medical Center
525 East 68th Street, Box 147
New York, NY 10021

Craig Tomlinson  

Previously uncited archival material, principally letters between Adolf Meyer and Sandor Rado, is used to document the importance of a relationship between two influential figures in American psychiatry and psychoanalysis at a fateful moment in the evolution of both. Although Meyer and Rado have each fallen into relative neglect, their interaction not only was a crucial part of the schisms in American analysis in the 1930s and 1940s, but represented an important juncture in the history of the relationship between medicine, academic psychiatry and psychoanalysis in North America. Meyer's powerful influence was thus an essential component of Rado's attempts to rewrite psychoanalytic theory and terminology wholesale. Moreover, Rado specifically integrated the results of a radically integrationist agenda he had consolidated in his contact with Meyer into the organisation and educational programme of the psychoanalytic institute he founded at Columbia University in the early 1940s.

[[RunningHead]]

Mythologie Grecque Et Psychanalyse [Greek Mythology And Psychoanalysis].

By Graziella & Nicos Nicolaïdis. Neuchâtel. 1994. Pp. 207.

Perel Wilgowicz

Perel Wilgowicz

Perel Wilgowicz
7, rue des Blancs Manteaux, 75004 Paris

Perel Wilgowicz  

Graziella and Nicos Nicolaïdis are members of the Swiss Psychoanalytical Society. They offer us a wide-ranging survey of Greek mythology, based on the interest of the inventor of psychoanalysis in the myths of Oedipus and Narcissus. They see myth as a ‘history of desire [that] offers itself as a formation of unconscious desires—like a structure—having a libidinal value for the people (group), in the same way as representations, dreams, fantasies or delusions for the individual’, and devote their book to the evocation of this kinship between mythology, Greek tragedy and psychoanalysis. In the characters of tragedy or the heroes, gods or demigods of the myths, they find the conflicts, forms of identification or projection, splits, defence mechanisms and sublimations revealed by psychoanalysis in the individual: ‘Both the individual and the people construct an apparatus, which is psychic in the case of the subject and ethnic and cultural in that of the people’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L'oeuvre Du Temps En Psychanalyse [The Work Of Time In Psychoanalysis].

By Sylvie Le Poulichet. Paris: Rivages/Psychanalyse, Payot and Rivages. 1994. Pp. 234.

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin
27 rue Guynemer, 93000 Drancy

Dominique Bourdin  

A lecturer at the University of Paris VII and a Lacanian, Sylvie Le Poulichet has previously investigated the problems of the clinical treatment of drug addiction, with telling discussions of these ‘narcoses of desire’ (Toxicomanies et psychanalyse, Presses Univ. France, 1987), and has also dealt with various aspects of fantasies about the environment.

In the present book she examines psychoanalytic treatment as a specific form of the linking of time—or rather as the emergence of times that favour the process of becoming a subject or show this process at work.

Her main hypothesis emerges clearly from the very first chapter: it is the idea of an ‘identifying time’ connected with the transference and with the present-day presence in the treatment of the history, that is, of a past which is not of the past. This identifying time constitutes and transforms the distinction between outside and inside and reveals the alien within the similar. ‘Repetition gives rise to objects’—to the rhythm of the transference, which is at all times a process of transition.

This pertinent thesis gives much food for thought; it is brought home by a full style of writing that evokes rather than demonstrates, in a kind of phenomenology of the treatment whose theoretical status is not always well defined. It is illuminated by analyses of the time of dreams and on the linking of times in the family romance; although these are in a way very classical, they inspire reflection, with their valuable alternation between the revisiting of Freudian texts and clinical description. A kind of meditation on human temporality thus arises. The pages on the ‘no-go area’ of an individual's origin, which undergoes constant recomposition, help us to conceive of the catastrophic instant, the moment when the linking of times proves inaccessible, thereby ruling out any recomposition of the origin and any possibility of becoming a subject.

Brilliance of formulation sometimes replaces the work of conceptualisation. The sparkling facets that make up the coherence of Part One come together in the second part in the form of two clinical studies. In the first, drug addiction, reinterpreted in terms of excess, exile and alteration, is seen as the invention of an ‘autochrony’. The second study is concerned with the cannibalistic time of bulimia and establishes a kind of genesis of fantasy.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Bertram J. Cohler</cs></ln><ln>University of Chicago</ln><ln>Committee on Human Development</ln><ln>5730 South Woodlawn Avenue</ln><ln>Chicago, IL 60637</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Panel Report - Psychic Reality And The Analyst: The Inner Working Of The Analyst's Mind: Chaired by HELEN C. MEYERS, New York
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report - Psychic Reality And The Analyst: The Inner Working Of The Analyst's Mind

Chaired by HELEN C. MEYERS, New York

Bertram Cohler

Bertram Cohler

Bertram J. Cohler
University of Chicago
Committee on Human Development
5730 South Woodlawn Avenue
Chicago, IL 60637

Bertram Cohler  

The Chairman introduced the panellists and provided a focus for the afternoon's discussion. She observed that we can only know what we experience consciously. Understanding the unconscious or inner workings of the analyst's mind is much more difficult. The question is whether there can be an objective material reality independent of our experience of that reality. Freud maintained that the important issue was external, objective, material reality, which he contrasted with psychic or inner reality. While Freud believed that psychic reality referred to unconscious drives, Meyers maintained that psychic reality must refer to the inner world of subjective, personally constructed representations and perceptions, both conscious and unconscious, influenced by, but not equivalent to the outer world and inner needs and fantasies.

The concept of psychic reality has often been overlooked (see Arlow, 1985; Michels, 1985). Meyers noted that while this term has been understood in somewhat different ways, the concept of psychic reality is essential for psychoanalytic modes of understanding and is more subtle and complex than it first appears. Subjective experience includes both conscious and unconscious knowledge, self and object representations, identity and fantasy. Initially, within psychoanalysis, focus was on the analysand's own psychic reality. More recently, we have become aware that the analyst, too, works on the basis of his or her own psychic reality. As a result, the study of psychoanalytic process has been enlarged to include both the analysand's psychic reality and that of the analyst as well.

While earlier studies, such as Annie Reich's classic paper (1951) on countertransference, focused on countertransference as a possible source of interference in the analytic process, more recent study (Racker, 1968) has emphasised the ubiquitous and inevitable contributions of countertransference founded on psychic reality as an advantage in fostering the psychoanalytic process. The present panel explores the importance of this awareness of the analyst's psychic reality and evaluates both the promise and problems introduced by clinical focus on the analyst's psychic reality.

responseSet.title: Panel Report - Psychic Reality: Its Relationship To Defences Involving Negative Mechanisms: Chaired by WARREN S. POLAND, Washington
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report - Psychic Reality: Its Relationship To Defences Involving Negative Mechanisms

Chaired by WARREN S. POLAND, Washington

Clifford Yorke

Clifford Yorke

Clifford Yorke
The Anna Freud Centre
21 Maresfield Gardens
London NW3

Clifford Yorke  

The Chairman opened the panel at this very full meeting and displayed the lively good humour that characterised his chairmanship. He proposed to call on the speakers to read their papers, and after that to open the discussion on this fascinating but complex issue.

The first panellist, Michael Parsons, began by discussing the ‘analytic stance’. This was not simply the attitude with which one approached the patient: it applied to the way one read analytic literature or had analytic thoughts. It brought with it a slight sense of tension, since effort was needed to stay attuned to psychic, as opposed to ordinary, reality. It meant saying ‘no’ to something to which we habitually said ‘yes’.

A patient once told him that she would wake in the night and tell her father that she was afraid of burglars, or of dying, or of nuclear war. His angry reassurance would make her more agitated, not less. He failed to meet her on the level, not of ordinary, but of psychic, reality, where her fears had substance. That would have meant negating his ordinary way of thinking. But for psychoanalysts, psychic reality was the level at which their work was done.

The paradox was reflected in recent controversy over psychoanalytic listening. Some believed we listened in order to detect and understand distortions in the patient's view of reality. Others thought the psychoanalyst was no arbiter of reality: his job was to help the patient to understand his own psychic reality better, not to correct it. Both arguments were persuasive because each focused on a different, though valid, aspect of psychic reality: the paradox could lead to new understanding.

He said that there was a paradox, too, in Freud's paper ‘Negation’. On the one hand negation was defensive; on the other, it enriched our thinking and freed it from repression, since it brought the mental content in question into consciousness. Likewise, something essentially defensive made analytic understanding possible. Resistance, transference and countertransference all arose in the realm of psychic reality and displayed this paradox.

responseSet.title: La Naissance d'Oedipe [The Birth Of Oedipus].: By Jean Bollack. Paris: Gallimard. 1995. Pp. 349.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La Naissance d'Oedipe [The Birth Of Oedipus].

By Jean Bollack. Paris: Gallimard. 1995. Pp. 349.

Perel Wilgowicz

Perel Wilgowicz

Perel Wilgowicz  

Sophocles's King Oedipus, translated into French and provided with a commentary by Jean Bollack, a philologist of considerable erudition and culture, is essential reading for any psychoanalyst today. The author undertakes a critical reconstruction of the interpretations of

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1996

421

the tragedy, taking account of the time and background of the interpreters' reading and the history of the text; he concentrates on the sense as fixed when the work was set down in writing, with a view to separating it out from the systems of thought underlying traditional scholarly discussion, whether ancient, Byzantine or modern. For him, philology has the means to acquaint us with the linguistic or social make-up of an era. The tragedy is more epic than myth, examining as it does the raw data of the myth in a distancing process that organises ‘the verbal space from which the dramatic action emerges’. The destiny of Oedipus thus revisited is no longer what it was when Freud laid down its essence for the psychoanalysts. By the innovative light Bollack casts on the play, he substantially alters the Freudian perspective, linking the history of the son of Laius with that of the House of Labdacus in a new way. In scrutinising his past, the hero of the tragedy goes back to the origins of the race that had cast him out; he is the heir of the history of the genos. Oedipus was born damned. He ought not to have been born.

If you are the man, O then your life is lost! (lines 1180-1181).

The first transgression was the marriage of Laius and Jocasta, which should have been childless because their offspring was doomed.

[Time] has found you and judged that marriagemockery, bridegroom-son! (lines 1214-1215).

In the author's view, the incest is made meaningful by the logic of regicide and parricide. The hero's political identification, creating a duplicate of Laius in Oedipus, the king and husband of Jocasta, has culminated in a double murder, involving the elimination of the father and the nullification of his fatherhood when Oedipus substitutes for him in the act of generation: ‘Procreation spills the father's blood, by reincarnating him in the act of begetting’. By marrying Jocasta, Oedipus kills his father over again and abolishes the life of his children, in a repetitive structure in which he brings about his own destruction and sunders the chain of the generations by superimposing them. He is the ‘legatee of destruction’. In the progeny of Cadmus and Harmony, a transgression that tends towards the reproduction of the identical has been transmitted: ‘Temporal succession has been abolished to the detriment of the race that lives only by that succession’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: Psychic Reality And The Psychoanalysis Of The Adolescent

Chaired by THEODORE J. JACOBS, New York

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

Aaron H. Esman
1100 Park Avenue
Suite 1C
New York
NY 10021

Aaron Esman  

In keeping with the overall theme of the Congress, the special issues encountered in engaging the ‘psychic reality’ of adolescents were addressed. The Chairman provided a conceptual background, detailing the challenges posed to the analyst by the developmental circumstances of the adolescent—his fear of regression, his acute conflicts over nascent (or resurgent) sexual/incestuous wishes; his defensive tendency to externalise and his primary focus on the external world and the living present, rather than the childhood past. He pointed out that all of these factors interfered with the analytic focus on the transference, generally considered today the sine qua non of analytic work. Adolescents, particularly younger ones, were threatened by both sexual and dependent feelings towards the analyst, and thus tended to distance themselves to a degree that led some analysts to consider analytic work with adolescents impossible.

In order to counter these trends, said Jacobs ‘the analyst of the adolescent must not only be flexible, patient and imaginative … but must have the capacity to enter the world of the young person and to live with him or her in that world. By living in that world, that reality, we hope to gain access to another world, the psychic reality of the patient’. The analyst must also have the ability to relive his own adolescent experience, and to address his resistance to doing so where it arises—to maintain access, that is, to the ‘psychic reality’ of his own adolescent years. It was precisely this capacity of the two presenters (Aryan and Zeug), he asserted, that made their reports both fascinating and challenging accounts of the possibility of coming to grips with the inner world—the ‘psychic reality’—of at least some adolescents in analysis.

The first panellist, Marietta Zeug, then described her work with a 19-year-old man (A) who sought analysis because of the crippling effects of the persistent fantasy that he had been injured and was paralysed from the waist down and confined to a wheelchair. This proved to be what Laufer has called a ‘central masturbation fantasy’, condensing a multiplicity of identifications, drive elements and defensive positions. The patient's parents had divorced when he was 6; his father had soon remarried and had three children with his new wife, with all of whom the patient felt intensely rivalrous.

responseSet.title: Learning Theory And Intrapsychic Conflict
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Learning Theory And Intrapsychic Conflict

Eric Gillett

Eric Gillett

Eric Gillett
25 Cypress Ave.
Kentfield, California 94904

Eric Gillett  

This paper is one in a series (Gillett, 1990, 1994) attempting to explore the implications of modern ideas about learning for psychoanalytic theories of treatment and pathogenesis. The key concept is that of learned expectations, which establishes links with Freud's 1926 theory of neurotic anxiety as caused by the expectation of danger. The new understanding of classical Pavlovian conditioning entails changes in the basic theory of intrapsychic conflict described in previous papers (1990, 1994). The relationship of learning theory to Freud's 1926 theory of intrapsychic conflict has received insufficient attention in the psychoanalytic literature because of insufficient familiarity with the repudiation of behaviourism by psychologists in favour of a representational theory of the mind.

[[RunningHead]]

The Motherhood Constellation: A Unified View of Parent-Infant Psychotherapy.

By Daniel Stern. New York: Basic Books. 1995. Pp. 229.

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman
1100 Park Avenue, Suite B, New York, NY 10128

Aaron Esman  

When Leo Stone composed his now classic paper, ‘The widening scope of indications for psychoanalysis’ in 1954, it is not likely that he thought of infants or infant-parent pairs as potential subjects. At that time, even child analysts assumed some capacity for verbal communication as an essential prerequisite for the clinical (rather than preventive) application of psychoanalytic methods. It was the pioneering work of René Spitz, John Bowlby, Margaret Mahler and above all Selma Fraiberg that provided the groundwork for the evolution of a field of infant psychiatry, now a burgeoning sub-speciality that has come to embrace a wide range of professional disciplines and theoretical perspectives.

Among the psychoanalytic contributors to this field, none has been more active than Daniel Stern. His earlier books, The Interpersonal World of the Infant (1985) and Diary of a Baby (1990)

responseSet.title: Trauma and termination
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Trauma and termination

Richard Waugaman

Richard Waugaman

Richard M. Waugaman
500 West Montgomery Avenue
Rockville
MD 20850

Richard Waugaman  

Dear Sir:

In her article, ‘Trauma and termination’ (Int. J. Psychoanal., 76: 51-65), Ferraro notes the ‘frequent occurrence of pregnancies at the time of terminations’. She goes on to say that ‘it is a subject neglected in the psychoanalytic literature’ (p. 61n.). Although she then cites two references pertinent to this topic, she omits a third reference: Jackel's 1966 article, ‘Interruptions during psychoanalytic treatment and the wish for a child’. Although he does not explicitly connect this wish with termination, his article is clearly germane to Ferraro's point. Jackel writes:

The many fantasies which occur in reaction to interruption in the analysis in connection with vacations, holidays, etc., have been frequently discussed in the analytic literature. It would seem, however, that one particular type of fantasy, which in the author's experience is common, has not been sufficiently explored. This concerns the emergence, under the impact of impending separation, of the wish to have a child. This wish may manifest itself in transference acting out, in dreams, in the appearance or reappearance of symptoms, and in the associations of the patient. It occurs in both sexes. Although overdetermined, it would seem to be primarily a preoedipal phenomenon (J. Amer. Psychoanal. Assn., 14, p. 730).

He also states:

Often the wish is to establish a mother-child relationship in which the patient is both mother and (by identification) child. In such a fantasy, one can never be deserted and one is never alone (p. 733).

Sincerely,
Richard M. Waugaman

396
