responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fixation Of Asthma And Sexual Impotence At Different Pregenital Stages

Salvador Adroer

Salvador Adroer

Salvador Adroer
Pl. Gironella 2-2
°
08017 Barcelona

Salvador Adroer  

Most psychoanalytic writers believe that both asthma and sexual impotence depend on fixations at the early stages of the Oedipus complex. Through psychoanalytic study of patients with both disorders, the author believes that this problem may be considered from another angle whereby the fixation of sexual impotence is situated at the early stages of the Oedipus complex, while asthma depends on fixations at the level of the breast although its character is also triangular. It is the author's opinion that in studying the psycho-pathogenesis of these afflictions it is important to distinguish between basic and defensive phantasies, since it is the former which reveals the level of object relations where the malady is situated. Defensive phantasies include, owing to regressive and progressive movements, objects and defences from other levels and therefore cannot be used for the investigation of levels of fixation. In the cases presented, the aim of the psychosomatic symptom is, in asthma, to avoid the collapse of the self while, in impotence, it is to avoid castration anxieties, when the introjected object does not provide sufficient cohesion to the self or is experienced as persecutory. At first the patient uses manic defences but should these fail, further defences are resorted to (contraction of the bronchioles in asthma and inhibition in sexual impotence), and these give rise to the psychosomatic symptom.

responseSet.title: Myth And Madness: A Report Of A Psychoanalytic Study Of Antisemitism
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Myth And Madness: A Report Of A Psychoanalytic Study Of Antisemitism

Mortimer Ostow

Mortimer Ostow

Mortimer Ostow
5021 Iselin Avenue
Riverdale
New York
NY10128

Mortimer Ostow  

This essay reports the findings and conclusions of a psychoanalytic study of antisemitism based upon case reports and classical, historical and literary documents. With respect to antisemitic sentiments, individual dynamics are overridden by stereotypical myths. After the definitive secession of early Jewish and Gentile Christ followers from the Jewish community at the end of the first century Common Era, the Jews were stigmatised and demonised by them and by the early Church fathers and labelled as a principle of evil, along with Satan, that was to blame for all Christian misfortune. The many antisemitic myths that evolved throughout the history of the Christian West all concurred in this theme. Apocalyptic thinking required such a principle as the source of the death phase, so that the elimination of Jews became the condition for the rebirth phase. In the presence of a sense of disorganisation and chaos, societies congeal into fundamentalist groups that require a mythic enemy. These groups tend to cultivate apocalyptic paranoia. Under those circumstances, anti-Jewish sentiment and discrimination become active persecution. The essay, and to a much greater extent, the book upon which it is based, examine some of the findings of the case studies, analyse apocalyptic thinking and describe the psychology of the fundamentalist community.

responseSet.title: Gill, Gray, Mitchell And Reed On Psychoanalytic Technique
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Gill, Gray, Mitchell And Reed On Psychoanalytic Technique

Robert Michels

Robert Michels

Robert Michels
Cornell University Medical College
New York, NY 10021

Robert Michels  

Psychoanalysis in Transition. By Merton Gill. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 1994. Pp. 179.

The Ego and Analysis of Defense. By Paul Gray. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp. 254.

Hope and Dread in Psychoanalysis. By Stephen A. Mitchell. New York: Basic Books. 1993. Pp. 285.

Transference Neuroses and Psychoanalytic Experience. By Gail Reed. New Haven, CT: Yale University Press. 1994. Pp. 252.

I

Psychoanalytic literature is increasingly centred on psychoanalytic process and technique, what transpires between patient and analyst, and how analysts do, and should, conduct analyses. The earlier dominant interest in psychoanalysis as a set of concepts and ideas about the mind or mental life—as a school of psychological theory rather than a method of psychotherapeutic treatment—has diminished. Today, even when the dialogue is conducted at a more general or abstract level, it is often only as background or context for a more clinical discussion.

II

The four books discussed here all illustrate this trend, although they do not all do so in the same way. Paul Gray's focus is the most narrow. For him, the basic principles of psychoanalytic theory are essentially axiomatic, to be understood and mastered, but not questioned. He sets himself the task of developing a clinical theory, a theory of technique that is faithful to that basic theory, and particularly of bringing psychoanalytic technique up to date with Anna Freud's 1936 monograph on ego-psychology, a contribution that leapt ahead of contemporary practice and, in Gray's view, has not even today been fully assimilated by psychoanalytic practitioners. Gray believes that many psychoanalysts continue attempting to overcome resistances rather than analysing them in accordance with Anna Freud's description of their psychodynamic structure. This lag in technique diminishes the potential value of the analytic experience for those patients who are appropriate for psychoanalysis (a narrower range than advocated by many of Gray's colleagues). His notion of psychoanalysis is captured by his statements that ‘the analyst's primary goal is always the analysis of the patient's psyche, not the patient's life’ (page 9), and ‘the theoretical and clinical framework within which I examine therapeutic action is the analysis of those problems that arise when instinctual derivatives, searching for more adequate discharge, encounter conflicts that force the ego to turn primarily to unconscious, rather than conscious, solutions’ (page 90).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: The Patient's Psychic Reality

Chaired by GILBERT DIATKINE, Paris

Bernard Penot

Bernard Penot

Bernard Penot
36, rue de l'Arbal
è
te
75005 Paris

Bernard Penot  

The Chairman opened the discussion and each presenter tried to establish some basic points. The first panellist, Eulália Torras de Beà, began with the fact that an analyst only grasps his patient's psychic reality through his own psychic reality. She suggested defining psychic reality as the internal representations of the external world and objects, and, I would add, by implication, the events that have occurred in life. She said this produced two pairs of opposites: the patient's psychic reality as opposed to another's (the analyst's) psychic reality; and psychic reality as contrasted with material (external) reality.

Two clinical examples were presented to show how the task of the analyst consists in taking into account and figuring out the kind of world the patient has inside him. The first case was that of an 11-year-old boy who sometimes appeared to be deluded. The boy spent many sessions drawing and describing a subterranean air-raid shelter—all this activity being aimed at mastering anxieties rooted in a dreadful internal world.

Her second patient was a young manager permanently frightened by the risk of not fulfilling his responsibilities. The material of the sessions was dominated by his fears concerning the possibility of his projects being copied through surveillance equipment, like microphones hidden in his office. At the same time, he was terrified of losing his wife through the revelation of a brief relationship he had had with a woman colleague. Therefore he felt he must take many steps to ensure that he would not ‘be caught and exposed’. In his sessions, he feared his analyst would make him ‘accept his reality, reduce [him] to his real position’.

In both cases, life was described as being unlivable. The analytic work consisted first in exploring and expressing, in the transference-countertransference relationship, the dynamics of the kind of world the patient had inside (atmosphere, weather, colours, relationships … ), so that it could be modified and his life could become livable. The patient's capacity for expressing a particular state of mind generally occurs when he is emerging from it.

[[RunningHead]]

Thinking, Feeling, and Being: Clinical Reflections on the Fundamental Antinomy of Human Beings and World.

By Ignacio Matte-Blanco. London: Routledge. 1988. Pp. 347.

James Grotstein

James Grotstein

James S. Grotstein
522 Dalehurst Avenue
Los Angeles, California 90024-2516

James Grotstein  

The extraordinary contributions of Ignacio Matte-Blanco have begun slowly—all too slowly—to influence a growing number of psychoanalysts around the world. In this and most of his other earlier works it seems that he has set out on a mathematical pilgrimage to the shrine of Freud's Unconscious often in a state of joyous rapture as he rediscovers Freud's genius and his inestimable legacy, not just the existence of unconscious mental life but the fundamental nature of its operation. Over and over again, Matte-Blanco reminds us that analysts have too long availed themselves of the harvest of Freud's discovery without sufficiently appreciating the complexity of the unconscious, particularly in regard to its laws, grammar, syntax, and hidden order of operation, the ‘strange attractor’ that mysteriously organises its ineffable functioning.

The author states:

I think it is fair to tell the reader that a thorough understanding of … this book requires a certain amount of work. As I see it, the main reason for this difficulty is due to the fact that it deals with the most difficult and yet most important aspects of the Freudian unconscious (pp. 4-5).

He is right. In my own attempts to master the thematics of this text I first read the enormously helpful Introduction prepared by Eric Rayner and David Tuckett. Then I read the main text, following which I re-read the Introduction. After that I re-read Matte-Blanco's (1975) The Unconscious as Infinite Sets and followed that up with a reading of Eric Rayner's (1995) Unconscious Logic: An Introduction to Matte-Blanco's Bi-Logic and Its Uses. Gradually I began to comprehend the scope and significance of this daring yet reverent tome. I shall do my best synoptically to describe its importance and fascination for analysts.

The terms he imparts to us are important definitory tools for our understanding the radically different logic of the Unconscious. They include in part: (a) bivalent (classical, Aristotelian) logic;

responseSet.title: The Empire Of The Ear: Freud's Problem With Music
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Empire Of The Ear: Freud's Problem With Music

Neil Cheshire

Neil Cheshire

Neil M. Cheshire
Arfon Mental Health Centre
26 College Road
Bangor LL57 2AN

Neil Cheshire  

Freud's difficulty in appreciating music, even though he seems to have been one of Charcot's ‘auditifs’ and had given auditory imagery a central place in his psychology, is re-examined in the light of his dealings with various distinguished musicians, and with special reference to the musical career of ‘Little Hans’. The author argues that Freud's exaggeration of his difficulty, combined with his ability to enjoy certain operas and his use of musical metaphors in the context of theory and therapy, confirms his own intuition of a conflict rather than a simple deficiency. This conflict is examined with reference to the theories of Eissler and of Vitz, and in the light of his own interest in classical Greek culture and in the nature of Art. Since opera was perhaps the only form of music that Freud could readily enjoy, the relation between words and melody in that genre is addressed. The significance for Freud of the specific works and passages that he mentions throughout his writings is examined in the light of some of his own theoretical concepts: (a) with special reference to ‘oedipal’ features, to the dynamics of ‘eros’ and ‘thanatos’, and to the balance between the ‘primary’ and ‘secondary’ processes in artistic creativity; and (b) as exemplified in his favourite operas ‘The Marriage of Figaro’, ‘Don Giovanni’, ‘Carmen’ and ‘The Mastersingers’. The parts played, in his problem with music, by his envy of the artist's intuitive talent for seduction and by his own ‘acoustic atrophy’ are also considered. He is defended against the recent charge that, in order to avoid having to cede primacy to others on points of psychology, he deliberately misrepresented how much he knew about music.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: Psychic Reality And Creativity

Chaired by DANIEL WIDLÖCHER, Paris

Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini
38 Berkeley Road
London N8 8RU

Andrea Sabbadini  

The Chairman opened the meeting by asking the general question of whether psychoanalysis provides a good paradigm for understanding creative processes. He then briefly introduced the four panellists and invited Ana Maria Andrade de Azevedo to read her presentation.

With reference to a quotation from Herbert Read, Andrade de Azevedo, the first panellist, wondered whether we should consider Freud's proposition to make conscious what is unconscious as the goal not only of psychoanalysis, but also of art. She saw the proximity between art and psychoanalysis in terms of form, that is, ‘psychoanalysis regarded as a performance in which knowledge is let loose’. In this sense, she considered psychoanalysis, as we move into the twenty-first century, as belonging to modernity, as a twentieth-century art of multiple languages; a method that ‘seeks to make available for psychic representation what had hitherto been only anxiety, pain, nameless terror’. Its object, according to Andrade de Azevedo, ‘has to do with the conquering and enlarging of areas of sensitivity, in short, to the area of subjectivity that constitutes the nucleus of what is called psychic reality’; this, she added, is always a creative act, as psychic reality is unknowable, unreachable and unthinkable unless through a process of representation which, by approximation and transformation, can attain some form of symbolic expression.

She then questioned Hanna Segal's definition of the creative impulse as restoration of lost or damaged internal objects, and suggested that a work of art (‘creation, not recreation’) will always be original and unique. With the help of two clinical vignettes, Andrade de Azevedo finally discussed theoretical and clinical creativity in the analyst, proposing that creativity can at times be feared ‘since it can be perceived as unruly, unpredictable and untidy whereas a more technical, objective and academic view would afford clarity and organisation’.

In the following presentation on ‘Multiplicity, paradox and the creative self’, the second panellist, Stuart Pizer, suggested that ‘psychic reality is not a singular island of subjectivity set off from the mainland of objective reality. Rather, psychic reality exists as the bridging of separate islands … of affect, memory, desire, introject, representation, word, metaphor, idea, mood, phantasy and vision that constitute the multiple components of self-experience’.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: Infertility, Surrogacy And The New Reproductive Techniques: Psychoanalytic Perspectives

Chaired by MARIANNE SPRINGER-KREMSER, Vienna

Salley Jessee

Salley Jessee

Salley S. Jessee
5064 Roswell Road, Suite D-201
Atlanta
GA 30342

Salley Jessee  

The Chairman introduced the panel by making the following points. She noted that in analytic work with infertile women who need technical assistance to reproduce, the interaction of psychic reality with external reality was important. Analyses of these women could be difficult for the female analyst whose own psychic reality was touched by the patient's dramatic struggles with fertility. Furthermore, a woman's submission to humiliating and painful procedures might unconsciously represent a lesser evil than infertility. Such a submission could also reflect psychopathology. Reproductive techniques have tremendous impact on psychic reality, whether the woman's infertility was caused by her biological condition, her psychic structure or by the interaction between the two. Springer-Kremser concluded her remarks by noting that the introduction of the infertility specialist as a third party in the analysis could further complicate an already difficult transference and countertransference.

The first panellist, Blanck de Cereijido, observing that the new reproductive techniques offered women the possibility of changing their lives, said that the use of these techniques posed important questions. What made women submit to painful physical procedures? What were the psychical consequences of having children in these circumstances? Surrogacy, as well as in vitro fertilisation, made us question convictions about the place of the mother. Was mother the one who contained the genetic code, the uterus that nurtured or the person who gave maternal care? Would the concept of mother continue to be linked to the concept of gender?

The desire for a child was not only a biological impulse, but an acquisition in the process of female development, Blanck de Cereijido emphasised. An understanding of this development enabled psychoanalysts to detect points at which intrapsychic conflicts could interfere with fertility. The desire to procreate depended on the culture to which the mother belonged. She pointed out though that despite the variety of cultural contexts, most civilisations had believed that a woman's identity equalled mother. The human desire for descendants gave a woman the possibility of a place in history, transmitting to her children the bio-cultural inheritance of their parents and ancestors. Procreation was also an attempt to ascribe meaning to life.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report Psychic Reality And Pathogenic Beliefs: Patients' Theories About Themselves And The Relational World

Chaired by HAROLD SAMPSON, San Francisco

Lucy LaFarge

Lucy LaFarge

Lucy LaFarge
239 Central Park West
New York
NY 10024

Lucy LaFarge  

The Chairman opened the panel with some questions about the role of beliefs in mental life: how does a person acquire beliefs about himself and the relational world? How central are beliefs that are pathogenic, causing maladaptation and psychopathology? Are pathogenic beliefs themselves the cause of psychopathology, or are they epiphenomena, the product of other psychic forces? What is their relation to wishes, fantasies and defences on the one hand, and external reality on the other? How do maladaptive beliefs change in psychoanalysis?

The first panellist, Joseph Weiss, arguing for the centrality of beliefs in psychic reality, suggested that the individual was highly motivated from the beginning of life to develop a reliable system of beliefs concerning the world, and particularly the people around him. These beliefs might be conscious or unconscious and they served as a guide to the expectable behaviour of the objects upon whom he depended for security. They were adaptive, even essential for survival. Pathogenic beliefs also arose in the service of adaptation, but while they served the individual adequately as a guide to his unique early experiences, they proved maladaptive later in development. Once established, he continued, beliefs became the central organisers of mental life, shaping strivings, affects, moods and behaviour.

Weiss emphasised the importance of external reality in the development of beliefs, arguing that the child developed a map of his objects based on his real experiences with them and developed maladaptive or pathogenic beliefs by inference from traumatic experiences with parents and siblings or learnt them directly from parental teaching. Weiss drew a clear distinction between beliefs and fantasies, arguing that beliefs were more fundamental than fantasies because they guaranteed survival.

Weiss argued that the concept of pathogenic belief could be used to explain behaviour that had in the past been explained in terms of primary unconscious impulse, defence, and fantasy. He illustrated this point with three brief clinical examples. He cited quantitative research to support the idea that patients suffer from pathogenic beliefs. They put their pathogenic beliefs to the test in the analytic or therapeutic situation, and experienced immediate and long-term benefit when the analyst's or therapist's interpretations helped to disprove their pathogenic beliefs.

[[RunningHead]]

Les Troubles De La Sexualité [Disorders Of Sexuality].

Edited by A. Fine, A. Le Guen and A. Oppenheimer. Paris: Presses Univ. France. Monographies de la Revue Française de Psychanalyse. 1993. Pp. 188.

Hélène Parat

Hélène Parat

Hélène Parat  

As the authors of its foreword acknowledge, this monograph is more obviously heterogeneous than its predecessors. While any such enterprise carries this risk, it seems that in this

* This review and the four that follow were translated by Philip Slotkin, MA, MITI.

Copyright © Institute of Psycho-Analysis, London, 1996

163

case, beyond the stylistic and theoretical differences between the authors and the diversity of the sexual disturbances considered, the heterogeneity in fact reflects the tension and breadth of the field. This adds to the interest of this multi-author work. After all, because the concept of sexuality is so central to psychoanalysis, it is impossible to discuss the ‘sexual disorders’ in the narrow sense (impotence, frigidity and perversions at different ages) without examining its pivotal function and its role as a fundamental reference-point, so that a treatment of the basic conceptions of psychoanalysis, its field and its implicit nosography is then automatically entailed. Sexuality is still the psychoanalyst's shibboleth, even if the manifest emphasis has shifted in the last few years to identity problems and the so-called narcissistic pathologies, while the disorders involving sexual symptoms that were the focus of interest of the early psychoanalysts have to some extent gone out of favour.

In ‘Le sexuel freudien. Une énigme originaire toujours actuelle’ [The sexual in Freud. A primal enigma that is still relevant today], Bonnet deals specifically with the symptomatic character of this (partial) ‘forgetting’ of the sexual as far as present-day analysis is concerned; reviewing the evolution of the concept and field of the sexual in Freud's oeuvre, he strongly emphasises its importance as a foundation of psychic functioning. But whatever the basic common ground shared by all analysts in regard to the shibboleth of the sexual, if we read Chasseguet-Smirgel's paper ‘Les religions du diable: quelques réflexions sur les significations historiques et sociales des perversions’ [The devil's religions: some reflections on the historical and social meanings of the perversions] and, immediately after it, Stoller's ‘Dynamique des troubles érotiques’ [The dynamics of erotic disorders], we observe that, while both deal with perversions, the sexual can be understood in different ways and even give rise to different forms of self-experience as a psychoanalyst. Stoller's contribution deliberately ventures off the beaten track with his provocative rejection of the difference between neurosis and perversion, but in his efforts to combine them and investigate the dynamics of erotic disorders, he seems to be once again overhastily equating the fantasy (mental) aspect of psychic functioning with that of sexual behaviour and acts. While Chasseguet-Smirgel takes the risk of testing her ideas against anthropological and religious material, she does so acceptably, respecting the specificities of the fields discussed and at the same time presenting an analytic interpretation that stresses the narcissistic insufficiency lying at the root of perversion and giving rise to a disproportion that endeavours to subvert any order or difference.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: Psychoanalysis Of The Child: Psychic Reality Of The Patient And The Analyst

Chaired by JOHAN NORMAN, Bromma, Sweden

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen

Alicia Etchegoyen
51 Limerston Street
London SW10 0BL

Alicia Etchegoyen  

The Chairman, Johan Norman, introduced the session by remarking on the asymmetry between the psychic reality of the child, which does not include the experience of being an adult and the psychic reality of the analyst's infantile experiences that are relatively unavailable to consciousness.

Norman described how the avoidance of psychic reality may be understood as an expression of ego defence mechanisms driven by anxiety or as an expression of an identification with internal objects that avoid or manipulate psychic truth. He suggested that the vicissitudes of psychic reality are particularly in evidence in child analysis and described the particular difficulties of maintaining an analytic setting which are inherent to child analysis. He referred to the significance of the psychic reality of the analyst, which must include the capacity to preserve a mental space where an integrated view of the sense of the setting and of the analysis is kept in order to allow the analyst to resonate with the child and infant like states being evoked in the transference and countertransference interaction.

The first panellist, Tessa Baradon, presented clinical material of the analysis of a 6-year-old-boy, Michael, who had been referred by his school. He was unable to cope with the academic work and was friendless. The eldest of two brothers, Michael came from a closely knit and intact family. Problems started at the age of 2, when Michael suffered a three-week separation from his mother, following his brother's birth. He was taken abroad to his father's country of origin. He returned an unsettled, anxious child, unable to separate and adjust to nursery school. Mother's relationship with Michael was intense and highly ambivalent.

Baradon postulates that Michael's problems arose as a result of deficit in achieving an enduring and integrated representation of the maternal object with a consequent split in his self representation. She further suggests that the abrupt separation at the age of 2 was developmentally beyond Michael's capacity to mentalise. The trauma resulted in a developmental inhibition of the process of using mental phenomena to give meaning to emotional experience. The consequence was a deficit in the structuralisation of the inner world, characterised by chaos, where boundaries were blurred and affect was unmodulated.

[[RunningHead]]

Letter

Gottfried Heuer

Gottfried Heuer

Gottfried Heuer
Professional Member,
Association of Jungian Analysts,
London
Member of International
Association for Analytical Psychology
13 Mansell Road
London W3 7QH

Gottfried Heuer  

Dear Sir:

I found myself able to agree with David Black's emotional reaction when, in his recent review of a book by Jungian analyst Rosemary Gordon, he wrote of

non-Jungian thinkers … almost invariably (being) quoted as having paralleled insights by Jung, which paradoxically creates a kind of irritation, as if Jung were a spoiled child who can never be left out of any congratulations (1994, p. 1292).

I have often been similarly irritated by claims for primacy in terms of who discovered what first. However, in more thoughtful vein, every once in a while I do feel that questions should be raised when an omission is all too blatant. I wonder why Winnicott's urging of by now over thirty years ago has gone unheeded. He wrote in his review of Jung's Memories, Dreams, Reflections (1963):

The publication of this book provides psychoanalysts with a chance, perhaps the last chance they will have, to come to terms with Jung. If we fail to come to terms with Jung we are self-proclaimed partisans, partisans in a false cause (1964, p. 450).

Jonathan Dunn's recent, excellent paper in this journal, ‘Intersubjectivity in psychoanalysis: a critical review’ (1995), provides us with a case illustration, so to speak. I admired and learned from his inspiring study of what has come to be called intersubjectivity. The bibliography of well over two full pages was most impressive. However, conspicuous by their absence are any references whatsoever to Jung's writings of over fifty years ago on this analytical phenomenon. In his book The Psychology of the Transference (1946), Jung discusses at length the following diagram:

This diagram demonstrates Jung's understanding of the analytical process. He deployed alchemical metaphors to underscore the interaction of both participants in the process. As Samuels puts it:

The thesis is that alchemical imagery is very well suited indeed to capture the almost impossible essence of analysis or any other deep, human connection: the play between interpersonal relatedness on the one hand and imaginal, intrapsychic activity on the other. Alchemical imagery not only permits the paradoxical nature of the interpersonal/intrapsychic seesaw to be grasped but does so in a way that acknowledges simultaneity, doing away with distracting substructure/superstructure division (1989, pp. 176-7).

Anne Denis

Anne Denis

Anne Denis
386 rue des Vennes

Anne Denis  

Dear Sir:

An error in Jankélévitch's French translation of Beyond the Pleasure Principle has had two consequences in my paper ‘Temporality and modes of language’ (Int. J. Psycho-Anal., 76: 1109-1119).

The first is an error in the French translation. The French sentence is:

396

Notre spéculation conçoit cet Éros comme exerçant son action dès l'origine et comme s'opposant, à partir du moment où la substance vivante était devenue animée, à ‘l'instinct de mort’ en tant qu'‘instinct de vie’ (Pte. Bibliothèque, Payot, p. 77).

The English sentence is:

Our speculations have suggested that Eros operates from the beginning of life and appears as a ‘life instinct’ in opposition to the ‘death instinct’ which was brought into being by the coming to life of inorganic substance (S.E. 18, p. 61).

The German is:

Die Spekulation lät dann diesen Eros vom Anfang des Lebens an Wirken und als ‘Lebenstrieb’ in Gegensatz zum ‘Todestrieb’ treten, dser durch die Belebung des Anorganischen entstanden ist (G. W. 13, p. 66).

Jankélévitch has translated Freud's phrase ‘die Belebung des Anorganischen’ as ‘la substance vivante … devenue animée’.

My failure to check the German text has compounded Jankélévitch's error.

Nevertheless, I think that this double error can be described as a felix culpa, as it seems to me that the hypothesis I developed from it, in which the emergence of the psychic (taking ‘animé’ in a metaphorical sense) is considered to enable the drives to be separated (and therefore to be opposed and fused)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Konkordanz zu den Gesammelten Werken von Sigmund Freud.

Edited by Samuel Guttman et al. Six volumes. Waterloo, Ontario, Canada: North Waterloo Academic Press. 1995. Pp. 6800.

Patrick Mahony

Patrick Mahony

Patrick J. Mahony
1297 St. Viateur, Outremont
Province de Qu
é
bec, H2V 1Z2

Patrick Mahony  

Only an astronomical metaphor suffices. In the field of Freudian studies, a star has just been born. Thanks to computer technology and the dedication of several researchers, we now have the Konkordanz, a complete alphabetical arrangement of every key word in the German edition of Freud's works, the Gesammelte Werke. The Konkordanz is indispensable for analysts and scholars who seek greater precision in tracing the development and disappearance of Freud's concepts and imagistic as well as metaphorical associations, and especially those figurative associations in which his theories are embedded. It follows that every creditable psychoanalytic society and any serious large library devoted to the history of thought should have a copy of the Konkordanz. While the cost of the Konkordanz is not negligible ($980), its informed use affords priceless advantages.

An immediate practical advantage of the Konkordanz is that, when checking a lapse of memory, one may quickly trace a word back to its forgotten source. A much greater, comprehensive advantage is that it provides an assured base for one to study Freud's terms and concepts in both a historical and developmental sense. Clinicians and researchers in the various disciplines influenced by Freud can now acquire a greater understanding of his contribution. Furthermore, they have a powerful instrument to dispel a morass of interpretive errors hitherto rampant in the psychoanalytic and academic circles and to make a wealth of new discoveries about Freud's psychic processes, both conscious and unconscious. In such a venture, we must engage neither in an infinite, trivialising exegesis nor welcome the illusion that since Freud wrote an essentially scientific discourse, its reductionistically called aesthetic features are peripheral and insignificant. Irrespective of conveying a distinct message from text to text, Freud's discourse, by virtue of its being typically composed under the pressure of an inner urgency, constantly conveys another meaning all of its own (Mahony, 1987a, b, 1989, 1995).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mind and Its Treatment: A Psychoanalytic Approach.

By Veikko Tähkä. Madison, Connecticut: International Universities Press, Inc. 1993. Pp. 490.

Jim Dimon

Jim Dimon

Jim Dimon
2116 Sutter Street, San Francisco, CA 94115

Jim Dimon  

Mind and Its Treatment is a large and ambitious undertaking by the leading figure in Finnish psychoanalysis. First, Tähkä presents his psychoanalytic theory of development coupled with his viewpoint about the way in which adult psychopathology is structured. Next, he presents his theory of psychoanalytic treatment, differentiated by the functional level of the difficulty (psychotic, borderline or neurotic). Tähkä's voice resonates most clearly with American ego-psychologists of the post-World War II generation and with the selfpsychology of Heinz Kohut. He is, however, a highly independent thinker, with, at times, refreshingly distinctive points of view.

Tähkä orients the reader in his introduction:

Psychopathology is seen essentially as resulting from arrest of structure-building interaction with phase-specific objects at varying stages of mental development. Correspondingly, analytic treatment is viewed as an attempt at activating and assisting a resumed structural development in the patient, with the analyst as a new developmental object.

Tähkä's developmental schema begins with a symbiotic infant, who is structurally in the position of the psychotic adult. Tähkä then describes the progression from self and object differentiation to self and object constancy. This stage correlates with the pathological position of the adult borderline patient. For Tähkä, this path to self and object constancy is characterised by incorporative and identificatory processes, particularly the ‘functionally selective identifications’, which support the pursuit of pleasure and avoidance of frustration. Once self and object constancy are (relatively) in place, additional identificatory processes come into play. First, there are the ‘judgmentally selected identifications’, which supply the child with desired characteristics of the parents for further development of the self. Then, there are ‘informative identifications’, which build a sense of shared emotional experience with parents. The inner worlds of the child and parent begin to connect for the child through these informative identifications. In Tähkä's schema, the adult neurotic has not successfully traversed this stage of structure-building. In discussing neurotic problems, Tähkä places special emphasis on the final adolescent resolution in the progression to a mature autonomy.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: The Contemporary Relevance Of Anna Freud's Contribution To Psychoanalysis

Chaired By Albert J. Solnit

Vincenzo Bonaminio

Vincenzo Bonaminio

Vincenzo Bonaminio
Via Nomentana 256
00162 Rome

Vincenzo Bonaminio  

The Chairman opened the panel, which was attended by about fifty people, and described it in his opening remarks as representing ‘part of a process of rediscovering and rediscussing Anna Freud's seminal contribution and influence in the field of psychoanalysis’. He stressed her lifelong dedication to the education of generations of adult and child analysts, and her deep influence in the development of a training programme in child analysis. He then gave a general frame of reference for Anna Freud's scientific leadership and said that her achievements could be viewed in three overlapping areas: (1) as a guardian and advocate of her father and his work in creating psychoanalysis; (2) as a pioneer in establishing child psychoanalysis; and (3) as a strikingly original natural scientist and psychoanalyst.

Solnit reminded the audience of S. L. Lustman's evaluation of her monumental achievements in psychoanalytic theory, technique and research methodology, in which research methodology is described as

the aspect of her thought which is least attended and insufficiently appreciated. The scaffold and texture of Anna Freud's writings represent the most proficient, knowledgeable, and functional grasp of the epistemological structure and function of theory—and may well represent her most influential long term contribution (1967, p. 810f.).

Stressing Anna Freud's inspiring scientific leadership and its rich legacy, Professor Solnit concluded his cogent introductory remarks and called on Riccardo Steiner to address the audience.

In his presentation entitled ‘A personal attempt to revisit Anna Freud's The Ideal Psychoanalytic Institute: A Utopia’ Riccardo Steiner aimed ‘to reflect on what we mean today by the internal and external time and space we all dedicate, have dedicated or would like to dedicate to being educated or to educating, and to learning more about improving our systems of preserving, transmitting and fostering psychoanalytic knowledge’. Obviously, he went on, the context in which we as analysts operate today is more complex than that in which Anna Freud put forward her ideas on this topic about thirty years ago.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: Psychic Reality And Films

Chaired By Harriet Kimble Wrye

Rosemary Balsam

Rosemary Balsam

Rosemary H. Balsam
64 Trumbull Street
New Haven, Connecticut 06510

Rosemary Balsam  

This panel on film and applied psychoanalysis was occasioned by the 100th anniversary of the invention of cinema and the founding of psychoanalysis. Panellists reported on Hitchcock's Vertigo (1958), Coppola's The Conversation (1974) and Citizen Kane (1941) by Orson Welles. Themes of gender and power were stressed. Analogies between the situation of the fictional protagonists and the role of the analyst were also elaborated. This event was a prelude to a Post-Congress weekend in Los Angeles, where analyst participants had a dialogue with movie-makers.

To mark the 100th anniversary of the emergence of both film and psychoanalysis, a three-part cultural and scientific programme, ‘Projections of Psychic Reality: Gender and Power on the American Screen’ took place in conjunction with the IPA 1995 Congress. This event-within-the-event was organised by the newly formed Coalition of Independent Societies of the United States in part to celebrate their new official connection to the IPA. The first part consisted of two films shown on evenings during the Congress, Vertigo, the 1958 Alfred Hitchcock classic and The Conversation made in 1974 by Francis Coppola. The second aspect of the programme was a panel discussion, reported below, about these movies plus Orson Welles's 1941 Citizen Kane (all three films being set in or near San Francisco). This panel was timed as the last one of the Congress, being a bridge to the third prong of the programme, a celebratory Post-Congress weekend in the American home of movie-making, Los Angeles.

Wrye chaired the panel session. She suggested a ‘need to create a language for a coherent dialogue between … [psychoanalysis and film]’. The central metaphor of her idea of reciprocity between the two disciplines was a love affair—not overlooking the various ambivalences. She noted chronological parallels in the beginnings of both. D. W. Griffith came to Hollywood in 1909, fourteen years after the Lumière brothers' first Paris film. Freud first came to the USA in 1909, fourteen years after the publication of Studies on Hysteria. Freud himself, of course, rejected the offer of the movie mogul Samuel Goldwyn to develop a great screen lovestory. This moment, however, did not detract from the fascination of the film industry with psychoanalysis and vice versa. Wrye constantly employed pairs of reflective ideas to capture the reciprocity she underlined between the two fields.

responseSet.title: Introduction To The Work Of Didier Anzieu
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction To The Work Of Didier Anzieu

Catherine Chabert

Catherine Chabert

Catherine Chabert
Universit
é
 Ren
é
 Descartes (Paris V)
Institut de Psychologie
28, rue Serpente
F-75006 Paris

Catherine Chabert  

SOME RECENT MAJOR WORKS

Le Moi-peau. Paris: Dunod, 1985; new edition 1995. The Skin Ego. A Psychoanalytic Approach to the Self, trans. C. Turner. New Haven and London: Yale Univ. Press, 1989.

L'piderme nomade et la peau psychique. Paris: Apsyge (with contributions by A. Ciccone, C. Gurin T. Nathan), 1990.

Beckett et le psychanalyste. Paris: Mentha-Archimbaud, 1992.

Le penser, du moi-peau au moi-pensant. Paris: Dunod, 1994.

A review of the work of Didier Anzieu as a whole reveals a seeming contradiction associated with the characteristic bipolarity of its contrasting elements. This oscillatory movement between poles constitutes a dialectic that is always organised around pairs of opposing concepts and as such resembles the Freudian system.

On the one hand, the reader is struck by the range and diversity of the fields explored, the plurality of methods by which they are investigated, the diversity of the topics and concepts examined, put forward for reflection as well as discussion, or created, developed, elaborated and expanded upon. On the other hand, the guiding strands are seen to exhibit a continuity: methods and questions recur; older works are republished, amended and updated; and earlier contributions are drawn upon for the purpose of transition to, and the opening up of, new thoughts and vistas.

These two aspects in fact turn out not to be mutually exclusive, because, while range and diversity are manifest qualities of the oeuvre, in-depth study of the various texts reveals a number of focal points that constitute a solid and permanent foundation. Close attention to the works of Anzieu discloses what may be the primal source of his epistemological approach, in which psychoanalysis is constantly developed anew in terms of a dialectic between inside and outside, namely, the author's passionate concern with the changing patterns of internal and external reality or, to be more precise, with the links between these realities and the elements that separate them.

Anzieu's originality always lies at the intersection of inside and outside. Starting from his main focus of interest, which is psychoanalytic treatment, he seeks out thoughtmodels elsewhere and attempts to integrate them within the analytic frame of reference.

responseSet.title: Subjects of Analysis.: By Thomas Ogden. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp. 230.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Subjects of Analysis.

By Thomas Ogden. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. 1994. Pp. 230.

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar
Department of Psychiatry, Jefferson Medical College
1201 Chestnut Street, Suite 1503, Philadelphia, PA 19107

Salman Akhtar  

After a period of relative intellectual stagnation, North American psychoanalysis is experiencing a new vigour (Cooper, 1983). Kernberg's integration of Kleinian ideas with contemporary ego-psychology, Kohut's founding of self-psychology, growing interest in the British and French schools, rediscovery of Ferenczi and Sullivan, enrichment of psychoanalytic developmental hypotheses by infant observational research, empirical investigations regarding the process and efficacy of clinical psychoanalysis and an overall atmosphere of theoretical openmindedness mark this rejuvenation. The intersubjective critique of the ‘classical’ model's positivistic tradition is the newest arrival on this exciting scene.

In the intersubjective approach it is asserted that psychic phenomena cannot be understood in isolation from their interpersonal matrix. Far from being discrete entities within the patient's psyche, mental events are dialectically constructed experiences, specific and idiosyncratic to a particular interpersonal situation or clinical interaction. Each glimpse of a patient's psychic reality is therefore contextual. It is inevitably coloured by the analyst's subjectivity which, in turn, is open to influence by the patient's inner experience. The attempt to unmask a psychic reality of the patient that is independent of a particular analytic interaction is therefore ‘epistemologically misguided’ (Dunn, 1995, p. 724) and clinically inadequate. Countertransference is not a technical error or impediment to clinical work. It refers to the totality of the analyst's subjective reactions, which provide invaluable clues to the patient's psychic life. Even more importantly, countertransference is not merely a reaction to the patient's experience but an active constructing force of the analytic material as a whole, including the transference. Neither is the analyst a ‘blank screen’ for the patient's projections, nor is the patient a tabula rasa for the analyst's interpretive dictates. The subjectivities of the two are interlinked and, in order to grasp the essentials of the patient's material as well as those of his own reverie, the analyst must keep this in mind.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Research in Psychoanalysis: Process, Development, Outcome.

Edited by T. Shapiro and R. N. Emde. Madison, CT: International Universities Press, Inc. 1995. Pp. 447.

Wilma Bucci

Wilma Bucci

Wilma Bucci
Derner Institute, Adelphi University
Garden City, NY 11530

Wilma Bucci  

This volume is an excellent collection of papers by leading psychoanalytic researchers, with commentaries highlighting the implications of the research for clinical practice. The volume, which initially appeared as a supplement to the Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association in 1993, contrasts with other important psychoanalytic research collections, such as those edited by Dahl, Kächele & Thomä (1988) and Miller, Luborsky, Barber & Docherty (1993), in including the areas of developmental psychiatry and psychology, as well as treatment research.

In general, we may see this volume as a testimony to the scientific advances of the psychoanalytic field, beyond the early experimental studies of psychoanalytic concepts, which are not included in this book, and also beyond the large sample outcome studies included here. With the new understanding of modern science, to which Emde refers, and the advances in psychological methodology included here, the papers as a group show how meanings and motives, which are central to psychoanalysis, may become objects of investigation in an empirical scientific field.

While the volume, with its broadly ranging selection of papers, necessarily has some aspects of a handbook or encyclopaedia, this review will focus on what I see as the central and shared themes. As Kernberg points out in his discussion, the focus on outcome was the domain of the first generation of psychoanalytic researchers while the emphasis on process is the domain of the second. The editors may be said to be representing a third generation, working towards the integration of developmental and treatment perspectives in an overall psychoanalytic science. I will discuss the papers from this integrative perspective, rather than following the—somewhat puzzling—order of sections in the book. I regret that this brief review cannot do justice to the many original and thought-provoking ideas represented in the individual chapters, each of which could potentially be the basis for a review of its own.

[[RunningHead]]

Freud, La Violence Et La Dépression (L'oedipe Et Le Narcissisme) [Freud, Violence And Depression (The Oedipus Complex And Narcissism)].

By Jean Bergeret. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 1995. Pp. 257.

Josée Violette

Josée Violette

Josée Violette
21 rue Guynemer, 93700 Drancy

Josée Violette  

After a long series of books by this author, several of them devoted to drug addiction and the treatment of subjects not conforming to the classical definitions of neurosis or psychosis but suffering from profound disorders of mental functioning and the internal economy, PUF have now published a collection of Bergeret's most important papers from the period 1980 to 1993. Associated with original texts and contributions to a variety of colloquia and congresses, they are organised to form a whole in which the development of the author's thought and hypotheses can be followed.

The book is divided into three parts, whose titles may be translated respectively as ‘Narcissism, the Oedipus complex and violence’, ‘Metapsychology examined’ and ‘The development of Freud's thought’.

Based on his wide-ranging clinical experience with a population of patients whose problems seemed inconsistent with Freud's theory of the neuroses, the author's research led him to put forward some hypotheses whereby the relevant mechanisms could be more readily explained.

responseSet.title: ‘Psychic Reality’ And Reality Testing In The Analysis Of Perverse Defences
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Psychic Reality’ And Reality Testing In The Analysis Of Perverse Defences

Alfred Plaut

Alfred Plaut

Alfred Plaut
Rezonvillestr. 1
12167 Berlin

Alfred Plaut  

Dear Sir:

Lee Grossman asserts that ‘psychic reality’ is fantasy (p. 509), and later (p. 511) equates it with unconscious fantasy. His aim is to prevent any blurring between outer, material reality and the imaginary (p. 510). Reality testing is said to depend on the patient's capacity to distinguish between the two, and the perversity of his (usually it is a male) defences is that he does not test or question these against the congruence of his own experience in the process of reality testing. This experience, in turn, is based on ‘the testimony of his senses’, which, we are told, would inform him what is real and what is imaginary. That is to say, perverse defences are a form of dishonesty; the patient discounts what he accurately perceives (p. 513). He does so for a well known reason, namely the avoidance of painful reality. To correct such self-deception, Grossman, in addition to interpretation, ‘also took up a position on behalf of reality’ (p. 514). In other words, he confronts the patient with the reality which he calls consensual and ‘material’. And this, according to the author, the patient is able to judge ‘by his own standards’. The implication is, however, that the standards are, or should be, consensual, i.e. the same as the analyst's.

Grossman does not tell us what the consequences of this technique are on the transference. What happens when the analyst supplements the patient's superego with the help of his own personality and authority?

Another open issue linked with Grossman's clinical observation concerns sexual perversions. If his definition of reality testing is right, masturbation must be regarded as a perversion because the fantasy accompanying it is an illusion, as the patient usually knows. Yet he prefers to leave reality untested as he reaches out for hallucinatory satisfaction. That is to say, we all behave like ‘certain people [who] take liberties with the testimony of their senses’ (p. 513).

Grossman places a great deal of confidence in the testimony of our senses on which the patient's perception of reality is said to be based. This taxes the reader who is aware of the unreliability of our senses in several respects. Our emotions as well as our bodies react to the illusions of art, notably the cinema, and other forms of simulated reality to the extent that we become oblivious of anything else. Among psychoanalysts it is particularly Bion (1963) who warns us against placing any trust in unworked-on or uninterpreted ‘sensa’. Reality, therefore, that is only tested by the patient's perceptions is unreliable. Yet Grossman reiterates that the patient is someone capable of testing his own reality and nevertheless takes licence to avoid responsibility for it. The analyst, on the other hand, must not abrogate his responsibility to ‘help his patient face the world as he or she knows it to be’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L'affect Partagé [Shared Affect].

By C. Parat. Foreword by A. Green. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, Collection ‘Le fait psychanalytique’. 1995.

Hélène Parat

Hélène Parat

Hélène Parat
71 bis, rue Cardinal Lemoine, 75005 Paris

Hélène Parat  

This book is the fruit of many years of demanding and passionate psychoanalytic practice. It is a collection of scattered papers published over a long period of time, but also includes critical additions and unpublished material, illustrating the course of an odyssey and the links between different aspects of the author's thought. For Catherine Parat, these links become increasingly clear in the acknowledgement of the importance of affect in both her theory and her practice. Affect, as the ‘area in which the representational lines are fixed’, is indeed the ‘living weft within which the links that construct psychic life are woven’.

Avoiding the pitfalls of a mere chronological compilation, the author has arranged her papers in groups of related themes (female sexuality, the Oedipus complex, the nucleus of the neuroses, clinical practice and analytic technique, the importance of affect and affect in therapy). This conveys something of the continuity of personal elaboration involved in a state of ‘being an analyst’ that entails constant progression, while at the same time highlighting her freedom with respect to certain imposed theoretical models and limiting ideals. The author is then able to produce a piece of ‘serene writing’, as André Green nicely puts it in his attentive and concise foreword. Green here clearly brings out the connections between the different focal points of the author's interest and discusses certain subtle aspects of her theory, such as the distinction between suppression and repression, in consequence of which allowance is made in the clinical situation for the disjunction between representation and affect. Catherine Parat's specific clinical experience, in particular with psychosomatic patients, has enriched her theoretical reflections, revealing a need for adjustments to and modifications of the setting; however, despite the subtlety of her analysis, her critical fidelity to Freud and her rigour should not be underestimated. The latter is demonstrated by her important contribution ‘The oedipal organisation of the genital stage’, in which she overcomes any limits imposed by a specific conceptualisation of the object relationship and develops a precise theory of the structural role of the oedipal organisation—i.e. the deferredaction reorganisation of the Oedipus complex in a new and unique but fortunately always precarious form.

responseSet.title: Panel Report: Multidisciplinary Perspectives On The Concept Of Psychic Reality: Chaired by DAVID M. SACHS, Philadelphia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Panel Report: Multidisciplinary Perspectives On The Concept Of Psychic Reality

Chaired by DAVID M. SACHS, Philadelphia

Stephen Grosz

Stephen Grosz

Stephen Grosz
52 Downshire Hill
London NW3 1PA

Stephen Grosz  

The Chairman began by describing the focus of this panel: some philosophical problems precipitated by the concept of psychic reality. In particular, the panellists would be presenting their views on a wide range of issues including psychoanalytic epistemology, the verification of interpretations which rely on a theory of psychic reality, some implications for psychoanalysis as a result of recent developments in the neurosciences, and some of the ways that philosophy can help us to think about ‘psychic reality’. Sachs then introduced the first speaker, Gregorio Klimovsky.

In ‘Psychic reality: some epistemological aspects’, Klimovsky sought to portray a few similarities between the aims of a scientific theory and those of psychic reality. In particular, both aspire to provide a description of empirical reality, but a scientific theory and a psychical description will always be inadequate because both must be partial and subjective. In other words, a scientific theory and a psychical description are both subject to the effects of the unconscious. Klimovsky put forward his view that the contribution of psychoanalysis lies in its discovery that the subject's reality involves objects which are not empirical in the sense of being located in consciousness, but are unconscious and a product of the subject's unique development. Such unconscious objects—phantasies, for example—are not simple representations of something external, they are a product of the individual's singular internal experience. Critically, these objects will have a causal relation to the individual's behaviour and experience of new conscious or empirical facts. In this way, psychic reality makes use of constructions which have their origin in the empirical world, and likewise, scientific theories make use of constructions which begin with an experience of the empirical world.

Klimovsky thought that while it can be argued that there is a difference between the structure of a scientific theory and that of a psychical construction (scientific theories may, for example, appear more rational or intentional), he thought that we would do well to look at some of the ways that the two are analogous. Turning to Hans Reichenbach's distinction between ‘context of discovery’ and ‘context of justification’, Klimovsky sought to show that there are strong similarities between the ways that a scientific theory and an unconscious psychical idea come into existence.

responseSet.title: Transference And Countertransference In The Analysis Of A Child With Autistic Nuclei
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transference And Countertransference In The Analysis Of A Child With Autistic Nuclei

Nilde Parada

Nilde Parada

Nilde Jacob Parada Franch
Rua Joao Moura 647, Conj. 103
CEP 05412-001
S
ã
o Paulo

Nilde Parada  

The author stresses the importance of using the countertransference when working with autistic and borderline children, in whom severe disorders of cognitive and emotional development have not permitted the establishment of an internal three-dimensional space whereby emotions can be contained, meaning can be assigned to those emotions and symbols can be formed. She argues that the transference will arise gradually in response to the analyst's countertransference. Great importance is attached to the setting in both its material and psychic aspects, the stability of the former being essential because of the patient's two-dimensional mode of functioning, in which he lacks any representation of space and time. After a review of the theoretical background to the treatment of autistic children with particular reference to the work of Houzel and Tustin and a brief discussion of general aspects of the transference in the light of the views of Freud, Klein and Bion, the author turns to the clinical material proper, presenting vignettes from a number of sessions in the analysis of an autistic boy. These show how she utilises her own emotions, sensations and associations to gain information about the patient's state and, by assigning meaning to these manifestations and communicating it to him, to impart motion to his frozen internal world. The author reports that, after several years of analysis, the patient has introjected a stable object that will lead to healthier development and a sense of security.

responseSet.title: Some Subtypes Of Depression And Their Implications For Psychoanalytic Treatment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Subtypes Of Depression And Their Implications For Psychoanalytic Treatment

Hugo Bleichmar

Hugo Bleichmar

Hugo B. Bleichmar
Diego de Leon, 44 - 3
°
 IZQ
28006 Madrid

Hugo Bleichmar  

The author argues the case for the need to develop an integrative clinical psychoanalytic model of depression which takes into account various pathways leading to different subtypes of depression. Some factors intervening in the genesis and maintenance of depression (aggression, guilt, narcissistic disorders, persecutory anxieties, ego deficits, masochism, identification with depressive parents and fixation to traumatic events in which the subject felt helpless) are examined, as well as certain dynamic interrelations between them. The relationship between aggression and depression is analysed, with particular attention devoted to the steps through which aggression turns into depression. The origins of guilt are reviewed, including those conditions which are independent of aggression. Psychoanalytic interventions that could be pertinent for a given subtype of depression, but which could prove counterproductive when applied to another subtype are discussed. Clinical examples are given to provide support for the ideas presented, recognising that further studies are required to establish more specific correlations between the different depressive configurations and the types of psychoanalytic interventions best suited for changing them.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Facts All Come With A Point Of View:

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper

Steven H. Cooper
875 Massachusetts Avenue, Suite 54
Cambridge
Mass. 02139

Steven Cooper  

In this paper the author examines some of the varieties of definitions of fact and formulation within contemporary psychoanalytic perspectives. A common thread running throughout various definitions of fact is the way in which fact is never theory-free. An attempt is made to compare and contrast the ways in which fact and formulation are contextually bound to theory. Questions are raised about the extent to which we can claim evidential status of propositions versus viewing formulations as useful illustrations of our work. Implicit in this paper is the current tension in psychoanalytic theory between psychoanalysis as a theory of motivation and meaning.

[[RunningHead]]

Some Views On Psychic Reality

R. Etchegoyen

R. Etchegoyen

R. Horacio Etchegoyen
Posados 1580 Piso 13
1112 Buenos Aires

R. Etchegoyen  

This paper is based on the Presidential Address given at the end of the San Francisco Congress in August 1995. The author concentrates on discussing the three main Panel presentations on psychic reality from the technical, clinical and theoretical perspectives and in the context of the life cycle. Referring to Grinberg's introduction to the Congress, he reiterates that psychic reality forms the central point from which psychoanalysis can be contemplated. A special mention is given to the Panel on multidisciplinary perspectives of psychic reality, which includes contributions from two well-known philosophers, in connection with the current epistemological status of psychoanalysis in the context of the Congress's main theme. The author concludes by quoting a sonnet, which he feels illustrates the complex dialectic between reality and fantasy, between truth and lies.

responseSet.title: An Adopted Analysand's Transference Of A ‘Hole-Object’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

An Adopted Analysand's Transference Of A ‘Hole-Object’

Danielle Quinodoz

Danielle Quinodoz

Danielle Quinodoz
53A Chemin des Fourches
1223 Cologny
Geneva

Danielle Quinodoz  

The author describes the vicissitudes of the transference and countertransference in cases where the internal object transferred by the patient on to the analyst is experienced by the former as non-existent. A case history involving a ‘hole-object’1 of this kind is presented, the patient concerned having been adopted at the age of six months and having the fantasy that she did not exist before her adoption. The hole-object is created by the patient to defend against psychic suffering and aggressive drives towards the object. A careful distinction is made between the hole-object, which is defined in terms of its non-existence, and the absent object, the ‘psychic hole’, the melancholic object and the bad-breast feeling, all of which exist or have existed at some time. The author describes the ‘double transference’, in which she represented both the idealised albeit well cathected adoptive parents and the disavowed, abandoning biological parents; in the latter case indifference took the place of love and hate in the transference. The analyst in this situation must in the author's view interpret his transference role as a hole-object so as to confer existence on this object and make it representable, and for this purpose a vital role falls to the countertransference. The split between the abandoning and the adopting aspects of the parents can then be resolved, giving rise to a single identificatory parental image.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Ronald Markillie</cs></ln><ln>Swallowfield Park</ln><ln>Swallowfield</ln><ln>Reading</ln><ln>Berks RG7 1TG</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Some Personal Recollections And Impressions Of Harry Guntrip
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Personal Recollections And Impressions Of Harry Guntrip

Ronald Markillie

Ronald Markillie

Ronald Markillie
Swallowfield Park
Swallowfield
Reading
Berks RG7 1TG

Ronald Markillie  

Harry Guntrip will be known to most readers, and a generation of students, as the author of ‘Personality Structure and Human Interaction’, and of ‘Schizoid Phenomena, Object Relations and the Self. These books are still an extremely clear and well argued presentation of the development of psychoanalytic theory. The first was an enormous labour of digesting, criticising and organising all the major psychoanalytic contributions into a theoretical whole that was a statement of faith by the author. The second linked some of the papers that continuously flowed from him throughout life. Both are a witness to his drive and his intellect. This paper is a memoir of almost thirty years’ friendship, and shows something of the man and the therapist who made the books possible. It gives some picture of his life, his personality and his problems with health. In conclusion Guntrip's paper on his analysis with Fairbairn and Winnicott is discussed. The author considers that Guntrip's forte, an understanding of the schizoid problem, has its roots in a personal limitation revealed in his experience of analysis. He needed to be in intellectual control, and had great difficulty in emotional letting go.

responseSet.title: Revenge And Resentment In The ‘Oedipus Situation’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Revenge And Resentment In The ‘Oedipus Situation’

John Steiner

John Steiner

John Steiner
28 Park Drive
London NW11 7SP

John Steiner  

The author considers an important form of ‘psychic retreat’ to be based on resentment and grievance. In this paper he discusses how resentments can build up and be nursed when the impulse to violence and in particular revenge is prevented from being expressed. The resentment leads to an impasse from which it is difficult for the analyst and patient to extricate themselves. A clinical example illustrating such an impasse is discussed. Then extracts from ‘Kidnapped’ by Robert Louis Stevenson are used to illustrate ways in which resentment can be relinquished and replaced with forgiveness. It is argued that the issue of revenge has to be negotiated before such a transformation can result.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analysis Of ‘Associating’‘Interpreting’ And ‘Words’: Use Of This Analysis To Bring Unconscious Fantasies Into The Present And To Achieve Greater Ego Integration

Luisa de Alvarez de Toledo

Luisa de Alvarez de Toledo

Luisa de Alvarez de Toledo
Argentina

Luisa de Alvarez de Toledo  

In this paper first presented in 1953 and now translated into English for the first time, the author gives an account of the phenomena that occur when the meaning of ‘associating’, ‘interpreting’ and ‘words’ is investigated. Her presentation is modelled on the manner of appearance and understanding of these phenomena. Because interpretation of a new aspect has always preceded its intellectual understanding, frequent repetitions will be observed, and one theme is sometimes adumbrated within another and developed more fully later. For all its drawbacks, such an exposition has the advantage of coming closest to reality. A schematisation would be premature and tend to conceal any errors, thus impeding subsequent understanding of the phenomena concerned. The author discusses the phenomena in their order of appearance. Although the order was the same in every case, she does not rule out the possibility that this might be because of her personal approach. The bibliography lists works that stimulated her interest and were particularly useful in the understanding and formulation of her work.

responseSet.title: Ladies Of Fashion: Pleasure, Perversion Or Paraphilia
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Ladies Of Fashion: Pleasure, Perversion Or Paraphilia

Arlene Richards

Arlene Richards

Arlene Kramer Richards
200 East 89th Street, Apt. 458
New York
NY 10128

Arlene Richards  

The author considers the reasons why interest in fashion is important in female development. Freud's comment that ‘all women are clothes fetishists’ and his explanation that a woman uses clothes to show that ‘one can find in her everything that one can expect from women’ are amplified and challenged by other authors and psychoanalytic evidence. Caper's (1994) focus on the erotic meaning of his patient's clothing is contrasted with the use ego-psychologists have made of ‘appropriate’ clothing and grooming as markers of mental health and intact functioning. A case of a woman who had a shopping symptom is considered. The roots of the symptom in her fantasy life, its replacement of genital satisfaction, its precursors in interactions with her parents and its use in enacting sado-masochistic fantasies is elaborated in the case material. A second case briefly illustrates the role ascetic refusal of clothing may play in some women's psychic lives. Use of clothing as a way of displaying the body, as an indicator of economic power, as an incitement to envy, and as a sexual enticement is discussed. Examples of understanding of the psychological aspects of interest in clothing are drawn from art, anthropology, sociology, political science and philosophy. The paper concludes with the idea that clothing and shopping for clothing can range from normal pleasure to paraphilia and leaves open the question whether it can be a perversion. Focusing on clinical material about shopping for fashionable clothing, the author shows how shopping can become central in some women's lives as it is important in many. It considers the boundaries between source of pleasure, paraphilia and sexual fetish as they are exemplified in women's attitudes towards clothing and their use of it.

responseSet.title: Freud And Ferenczi: Trauma And Transference Depression
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud And Ferenczi: Trauma And Transference Depression

Thierry Bokanowski

Thierry Bokanowski

Thierry Bokanowski
48, rue des Francs-Bourgeois
75003 Paris

Thierry Bokanowski  

The author recounts how Ferenczi's original technical and theoretical advances in the treatment of ‘difficult’ cases have become part of the stock-in-trade of psychoanalysis and its metapsychology despite the fact that Freud took issue in particular with Ferenczi's re-evaluation of the concept of trauma. Drawing liberally on the published correspondence, the author contends that these advances stemmed from Ferenczi's own need to work through his mother complex, which had not been adequately tackled in his analysis with Freud and led to his reproaches to Freud for not having analysed his negative transference and his reactivated primary depression, which later became transference depression. These difficulties drove him to distress, confusion and hate. The author considers that Ferenczi introduced new concepts in the hope of extracting himself from this transference impasse. His helplessness led to the ‘wise baby’; his confusion yielded the ‘confusion of tongues’; and, lastly, his hate produced the ‘introjection of the adult's guilt’, arousing in the child the ‘terrorism of suffering’, ‘splitting’ and ‘fragmentation’.

responseSet.title: The Sense Of Solitude In The Psychoanalytic Encounter
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Sense Of Solitude In The Psychoanalytic Encounter

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Jean-Michel Quinodoz

Jean-Michel Quinodoz
53A, chemin des Fourches
1223 Cologny/Geneva

Jean-Michel Quinodoz  

The author emphasises the importance of the sense of solitude for the integration of psychic life in both analysand and analyst. This complex affect is stated to accompany different levels of anxiety that can be worked through in the transference. While the normal sense of solitude is a manifestation of relative maturity, the author shows that it is not readily tolerated by some analysands when, in the form of a painful sense of loneliness, it signals the approach of the depressive position and of psychic integration. A discussion of the concept in the work of Freud, Klein and Winnicott is followed by a clinical sequence showing how a female patient uses projective identification to transfer intolerable transference separation feelings on to the analyst, who then experiences them as his own. When the analyst succeeds in distinguishing between the pathological aspect (depression) and the healthy side (psychic pain due to the prospect of integration) of these affects, he is able to interpret. The author draws attention to the dangers of acting out by both partners in the analysis if this is not possible. The author deems it important for the psychoanalyst to have acquired a well-developed sense of solitude and insists that particular consideration be devoted to its presence in prospective candidates for psychoanalytic training.

Adam Limentani

Adam Limentani

London N12 8UD

Adam Limentani  

Originally prepared as part of the background material for an International Psychoanalytical Association Symposium devoted to a critical consideration and review of the structure and functioning of the IPA in 1988, this paper uses sources within the IPA's Archives to trace the key developments in their history since their foundation in 1910. An addendum provides some details of similar developments from 1985 to the present.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Fate Of Training Cases

Robert Glick , Paula Eagle , Bruce Luber and Steven Roose

Steven Roose

Robert Glick
Paula Eagle
Bruce Luber
Steven Roose
722 West 168th Street
—
P.I. 98
New York, NY 10032

Steven Roose  

The authors regard the treatment of analytic patients as a crucial, formative and ultimately integrative experience in a candidate's development that has received remarkably little direct attention as an aspect of analytic education. From 1983 to 1992 a questionnaire was sent to all graduates of the Columbia University Psychoanalytic Center for Training and Research asking for information on training cases, both those terminated before graduation and those still ongoing at the time of graduation. The authors report data on pre-graduation candidate case experience, treatment duration of training cases, the impact of graduation on the course of analysis and on a global assessment of the outcome. 70 per cent of the 71 who graduated between 1983 and 1992 returned the questionnaire. The survey showed that before graduation the average candidate had a cumulative nine-year experience of treating training cases. 35 per cent of cases terminated before graduation and were invariably considered to have an unsuccessful outcome. Thus the ‘failed case’ was a common event and should be anticipated as part of a candidate's education. There was no evidence of precipitous termination of cases after graduation; in fact the data suggest that graduation has no discernible effect on the timing of termination. The vast majority of candidates at Columbia continue to be supervised after graduation, which suggests that graduation is only a marker in the training of an analyst, rather than the point of completion. With respect to outcome, 23 per cent of the 151 terminated cases were rated successful. Though this finding is consistent with previous reports, the methodological limitations of this study limit the confidence in this result. One of the important issues raised by these results is the impact of training requirements on the candidate's education and the Fate of Training Cases.

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Jacob A. Arlow</cs></ln><ln>94 Wildwood Road</ln><ln>Great Neck</ln><ln>NY 11024</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Concept Of Psychic Reality—How Useful?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Concept Of Psychic Reality—How Useful?

Jacob Arlow

Jacob Arlow

Jacob A. Arlow
94 Wildwood Road
Great Neck
NY 11024

Jacob Arlow  

The author argues that Freud coined the phrase ‘psychic reality’ to indicate how neurotic patients and others treat unconscious psychic concepts and fears as if they were

real. They respond to the challenges of everyday life not in terms of objective reality, but rather in keeping with their inner ‘psychic’ reality. With the development of different schools of thought in psychoanalysis, opinions as to precisely what constitutes the contents and character of unconscious mental functioning differ enormously, to the extent that there is no general agreement as to the nature and quality of unconscious mental elements. Psychoanalysts do not have a shared view of what constitutes psychic reality, with the result

that genuine scientific interchange has become increasingly difficult and attenuated. The author considers that what is required is the exploration and enunciation of an agreed upon methodological approach to the interpretation of clinical data and thence to the formulation of mutually acceptable psychoanalytic concepts.
